stem: A 31-year-old man is undergoing emergent exploratory laparotomy for hemorrhage after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He has received 6 units of packed red blood cells, 4 units of plasma, and 3 L of crystalloid. Two hours into the case the surgeon notes persistent diffuse oozing from raw surfaces despite a normal platelet count and an absence of a discrete bleeding vessel. The core (esophageal) temperature is 33.4°C, arterial pH is 7.21, and ionized calcium is 1.05 mmol/L. The room temperature is 19°C and the patient is covered only with a single cotton blanket. Which intervention will most directly correct the contribution of this patient's physiology to ongoing nonsurgical bleeding?

choices.text: Transfuse two additional units of platelets to raise the count above 100,000/microliter.
choices.rationale: The platelet count is already normal and the coagulopathy is driven by enzyme dysfunction rather than thrombocytopenia, so additional platelets do not address the underlying problem.

choices.text: Aggressively rewarm the patient with forced-air warming, a fluid warmer, and raising the ambient room temperature.
choices.rationale: Hypothermia (33.4°C) impairs the kinetics of coagulation enzymes and platelet function and is a core component of the lethal triad, so active rewarming most directly reverses the temperature-dependent coagulopathy causing the diffuse oozing.

choices.text: Administer 1 g of intravenous calcium chloride to treat the measured ionized calcium.
choices.rationale: An ionized calcium of 1.05 mmol/L is only mildly reduced and is not the principal driver of this coagulopathy, so calcium repletion is reasonable supportive care but will not be the dominant correction.

choices.text: Give an empiric dose of intravenous sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidosis to a normal pH.
choices.rationale: Bicarbonate does not durably reverse the metabolic acidosis of ongoing hypoperfusion and bolus alkali therapy has not been shown to improve trauma coagulopathy or outcomes.

choices.text: Administer recombinant activated factor VII as first-line therapy for the oozing.
choices.rationale: Recombinant factor VIIa is not effective in a hypothermic, acidotic patient because its enzymatic activity is temperature- and pH-dependent and it carries thrombotic risk, making it inappropriate before the metabolic milieu is corrected.

explanation: Trauma patients develop a self-perpetuating lethal triad of hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy. Hypothermia slows the enzymatic reactions of the coagulation cascade and impairs platelet function, producing diffuse nonsurgical oozing even when platelet counts and factor levels appear adequate. Because the temperature-dependent enzymes only work efficiently near 37°C, products such as recombinant factor VIIa and even transfused clotting factors lose efficacy until the patient is rewarmed, so active rewarming is the highest-yield intervention. Calcium repletion and avoiding further crystalloid are useful adjuncts, but correcting hypothermia targets the dominant mechanism.
subtopic: Trauma lethal triad — hypothermia-induced coagulopathy and active rewarming

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Classic trauma lethal-triad vignette (hypothermia 33.4°C, acidosis pH 7.21, diffuse nonsurgical oozing with normal platelet count). Keyed answer B (active rewarming with forced-air, fluid warmer, raising the 19°C ambient temp — the cotton-blanket detail is a deliberate clue) is the single best choice: among the options, hypothermia is the most directly correctable driver of the temperature-dependent coagulopathy, slowing enzyme kinetics and impairing platelet function. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A is wrong (platelets already normal); C (iCa 1.05 only mildly low, ~normal 1.1-1.3) is a reasonable adjunct but not dominant; D (bolus bicarbonate does not durably correct perfusion acidosis or improve trauma coagulopathy outcomes); E (rFVIIa is temperature/pH-dependent, thrombotic, not first-line before metabolic correction). Rationales are accurate and current; the only minor nuance is that hypothermic coagulopathy partly reflects an in-vitro assay artifact (tests run at 37°C), but the in-vivo enzyme/platelet impairment is real and the explanation is not materially wrong. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545239/ resolves to 'Hypothermia - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf', matching reference_title exactly. It covers hypothermia, temperature-dependence of coagulation, platelet dysfunction, and rewarming modalities — on-topic and supports the teaching point. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 58-year-old woman with no cardiac history is recovering in the PACU after open reduction and internal fixation of a femur fracture sustained in a fall. Intraoperatively she received 2 units of packed red blood cells and 2 units of plasma for blood loss. Ninety minutes after the second unit of plasma began, she develops acute dyspnea, an SpO2 of 84% on 6 L nasal cannula, and bilateral diffuse crackles. Her temperature is 38.6°C, heart rate 118/min, and blood pressure 92/54 mmHg. A portable chest radiograph shows bilateral diffuse pulmonary infiltrates. The jugular venous pressure is not elevated, and a bedside echocardiogram shows normal left ventricular function with no volume overload. Which of the following best explains this presentation and the appropriate management?

choices.text: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload; treat with aggressive intravenous furosemide and continued transfusion of the pending unit.
choices.rationale: Circulatory overload typically produces hypertension, an elevated jugular venous pressure, and evidence of volume overload on echocardiography, none of which are present here, and continuing transfusion would be dangerous.

choices.text: An IgE-mediated anaphylactic transfusion reaction; treat with intramuscular epinephrine and antihistamines.
choices.rationale: Anaphylaxis presents with urticaria, bronchospasm, angioedema, and hypotension rather than fever with bilateral infiltrates and a normal-filling-pressure picture, so this does not fit the clinical syndrome.

choices.text: Acute coronary syndrome with flash pulmonary edema; treat with nitroglycerin and urgent cardiac catheterization.
choices.rationale: She has no cardiac history, preserved left ventricular function, and no signs of cardiogenic edema, making an ischemic cause of the bilateral infiltrates very unlikely.

choices.text: Transfusion-related acute lung injury; stop the transfusion and provide supportive lung-protective oxygenation and ventilation without diuresis.
choices.rationale: Hypoxemia with bilateral noncardiogenic pulmonary infiltrates and fever within 6 hours of a plasma-rich product, with normal cardiac filling, defines TRALI, which is managed by halting the transfusion and providing supportive lung-protective care rather than diuresis.

choices.text: Septic shock from a bacterially contaminated unit; begin broad-spectrum antibiotics and large-volume crystalloid resuscitation.
choices.rationale: Although fever and hypotension can occur with contamination, the defining feature here is acute noncardiogenic pulmonary edema with diffuse infiltrates, and large-volume crystalloid would worsen the lung injury rather than treat the true diagnosis.

explanation: TRALI is acute hypoxemia with bilateral noncardiogenic pulmonary edema occurring within 6 hours of transfusion, classically after plasma-containing or platelet products carrying donor anti-leukocyte antibodies. The key distinction from TACO is the absence of volume overload: normal or low jugular venous pressure, preserved cardiac function, and infiltrates that are not from hydrostatic edema, often accompanied by fever and transient leukopenia. Management is to stop the transfusion immediately and provide supportive lung-protective oxygenation and ventilation; diuretics are reserved for TACO and can be harmful in TRALI, which is not a volume-overload state.
subtopic: Massive transfusion complications — distinguishing TRALI from TACO
reference_title: Managing Fresh-Frozen Plasma Transfusion Adverse Effects: Allergic Reactions, TACO, and TRALI - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (D, TRALI) is the single best answer. The stem provides the classic TRALI picture: acute hypoxemia with bilateral noncardiogenic pulmonary infiltrates within 6 hours of a plasma-rich product (90 min after plasma started), with explicitly normal JVP, preserved LV function, and no volume overload — plus fever and hypotension, which are well-documented TRALI features. Management (stop transfusion, supportive lung-protective oxygenation/ventilation, avoid diuresis) is correct per the 2019 consensus definition. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A (TACO) requires volume overload/elevated JVP, both absent and the stem rules them out; B (anaphylaxis) would show urticaria/angioedema/bronchospasm without fever or bilateral infiltrates; C (ACS/flash edema) is hydrostatic/cardiogenic and incompatible with normal filling pressures and preserved LV; E (septic contamination) is the strongest distractor given fever+hypotension, but bacterial contamination is far more common with platelets than plasma, and the dominant feature is acute noncardiogenic pulmonary edema with bilateral infiltrates and normal cardiac filling — the TRALI syndrome, not a contaminated-unit picture; large-volume crystalloid would worsen lung injury. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK597370/ resolves to the StatPearls chapter 'Managing Fresh-Frozen Plasma Transfusion Adverse Effects: Allergic Reactions, TACO, and TRALI,' which directly covers the TRALI/TACO distinction and TRALI management — precisely on-topic and matches the cited title.

stem: A 44-year-old man is in the surgical ICU on the first night after a damage-control laparotomy for blunt abdominal trauma; his abdomen was packed and left open with a temporary closure, but the surgical team elected to formally close the fascia at the end of the case after bleeding appeared controlled. He has received large-volume crystalloid and blood products. Over the past two hours he has become progressively oliguric (urine output 8 mL/hr), his peak inspiratory pressures on volume-controlled ventilation have climbed from 28 to 42 cmH2O, and his abdomen is tensely distended. Blood pressure is 86/58 mmHg on rising norepinephrine, central venous pressure is 22 mmHg, and a bladder-pressure measurement is 28 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Take the patient back for surgical abdominal decompression with a temporary open-abdomen (laparostomy) closure.
choices.rationale: Sustained intra-abdominal pressure above 20 mmHg with new organ dysfunction (oliguria, rising airway pressures, hypotension) is abdominal compartment syndrome, and decompressive laparotomy with an open abdomen is the definitive treatment when it develops after fascial closure.

choices.text: Increase the norepinephrine infusion and add vasopressin to restore the mean arterial pressure.
choices.rationale: Escalating vasopressors treats a downstream symptom while the elevated intra-abdominal pressure continues to compress the inferior vena cava and renal vasculature, so the organ dysfunction will persist until the abdomen is decompressed.

choices.text: Administer a large crystalloid bolus to raise the central venous pressure and improve renal perfusion.
choices.rationale: The central venous pressure is falsely elevated by transmitted intra-abdominal pressure and additional fluid worsens visceral edema and raises intra-abdominal pressure further, aggravating the compartment syndrome.

choices.text: Increase PEEP and tidal volume to overcome the high peak inspiratory pressures and improve oxygenation.
choices.rationale: The elevated airway pressures arise from cephalad displacement of the diaphragm by abdominal hypertension, so increasing ventilator pressures does not address the cause and risks barotrauma.

choices.text: Start continuous renal replacement therapy to manage the oliguria and remove volume.
choices.rationale: Renal replacement may eventually be needed, but the oliguria is being driven by renal vein and parenchymal compression from intra-abdominal hypertension, which must be relieved surgically first.

explanation: Abdominal compartment syndrome is defined as a sustained intra-abdominal pressure greater than 20 mmHg accompanied by new organ dysfunction, and bladder (intravesical) pressure is the validated bedside surrogate. It classically arises after damage-control trauma when the fascia is closed in the setting of bowel edema, packing, and large-volume resuscitation, producing oliguria from renal compression, elevated airway pressures from diaphragmatic splinting, and hypotension from impaired venous return. Note that an elevated central venous pressure is misleading because intra-abdominal pressure is transmitted to the thorax. Definitive treatment is surgical decompression with a temporary open abdomen; medical measures alone do not relieve the mechanical cause.
subtopic: Damage-control surgery complications — abdominal compartment syndrome and decompression
reference_title: Abdominal Compartment Syndrome - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: Clinical: The stem is a textbook presentation of abdominal compartment syndrome after damage-control laparotomy with fascial closure — bladder pressure 28 mmHg (IAP >20) plus new multi-organ dysfunction (oliguria, rising peak airway pressures from diaphragmatic splinting, refractory hypotension from impaired venous return) meets the WSACS definition. The keyed answer (decompressive laparotomy with temporary open abdomen) is the unambiguous, definitive treatment, especially since the fascia was just closed. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: escalating vasopressors, a crystalloid bolus (harmful, worsens IAP; CVP falsely elevated by transmitted IAP), increasing PEEP/tidal volume (risks barotrauma), and CRRT all address downstream symptoms without relieving the mechanical cause. Notably, no distractor represents legitimate WSACS medical management (NMB, NG/rectal decompression, fluid removal), so there is no defensible non-surgical alternative — the question is not ambiguous. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. Reference: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430932/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Abdominal Compartment Syndrome' chapter, which confirms the IAP >20 mmHg + new organ dysfunction definition, bladder pressure as gold-standard measurement, damage-control surgery and massive resuscitation as causes, multi-organ effects, and escalation to surgical decompression. Citation is on-topic and supports the keyed content.

stem: A 64-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history and COPD (FEV1 55% predicted) is undergoing a right upper lobectomy via thoracotomy. A left-sided double-lumen tube is placed and confirmed by fiberoptic bronchoscopy, and one-lung ventilation of the left lung is initiated in the left lateral decubitus position. Twenty minutes later, with the surgeon working in the open chest, the SpO2 drifts from 99% to 84%. Peak airway pressures are unchanged, breath sounds are present over the dependent lung, and the FiO2 is already 1.0. Repeat fiberoptic bronchoscopy confirms the tube is correctly positioned with no secretions. What is the most appropriate next intervention to improve oxygenation?

choices.text: Apply 5-10 cm H2O of CPAP with oxygen to the nondependent (operative) right lung
choices.rationale: Correct: after confirming tube position and FiO2 1.0, applying CPAP to the collapsed nondependent lung recruits alveoli and improves oxygenation while only minimally interfering with surgical exposure.

choices.text: Immediately resume two-lung ventilation and ask the surgeon to pause
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this is the rescue maneuver for refractory or severe hypoxemia after lesser measures fail, not the first step when a graded, less disruptive option is available.

choices.text: Increase the dependent-lung tidal volume to 12 mL/kg to improve overall minute ventilation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: large tidal volumes to the dependent lung raise its vascular resistance, divert blood flow to the nonventilated lung, and worsen shunt rather than improving oxygenation.

choices.text: Add 15 cm H2O of PEEP to the dependent lung as the sole maneuver
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while modest dependent-lung PEEP can help, excessive PEEP overdistends dependent alveoli and shifts perfusion to the nonventilated lung, and CPAP to the operative lung is the more reliably effective first step.

choices.text: Administer a fluid bolus and start a phenylephrine infusion to raise systemic blood pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the problem is intrapulmonary shunt through the collapsed lung, not hypotension, and raising systemic pressure does not address the shunt fraction.

explanation: Hypoxemia during one-lung ventilation is driven by transpulmonary shunt through the nonventilated, nonperfused-but-still-perfused lung. After confirming correct tube position, adequate FiO2, and ruling out secretions or malposition, the most effective and least disruptive maneuver is to apply CPAP (typically 5-10 cm H2O) of oxygen to the nondependent operative lung, which recruits some alveoli and reduces shunt. PEEP to the dependent lung and ultimately resumption of two-lung ventilation are reserved for refractory cases. Large dependent-lung tidal volumes are counterproductive because they increase dependent pulmonary vascular resistance and divert flow to the shunting lung.
subtopic: Thoracic anesthesia: one-lung ventilation and management of intraoperative hypoxemia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (CPAP 5-10 cm H2O of oxygen to the nondependent/operative lung) is the single best answer and the classic board-correct first-line maneuver for OLV hypoxemia after confirming DLT position, FiO2 1.0, and ruling out secretions/malposition — it recruits the collapsed lung and reduces shunt with minimal surgical disruption. All distractors are genuinely inferior: resuming two-lung ventilation is correctly framed as the rescue step for refractory hypoxemia; increasing dependent-lung TV to 12 mL/kg is counterproductive (raises dependent PVR, diverts flow to the shunting lung, and violates lung-protective ventilation); 15 cm H2O PEEP as sole maneuver risks overdistension/perfusion diversion and is less reliable than CPAP; and fluid/phenylephrine does not address intrapulmonary shunt. Rationales are accurate and current. Minor nitpick: the explanation contains an awkward garbled clause ('nonperfused-but-still-perfused lung'), but the physiology conveyed (shunt through the collapsed, incompletely HPV-diverted lung) is correct and this is not a medical error and does not appear in any answer choice. REFERENCE: WebFetched https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538314/ — resolves to StatPearls 'Single-Lung Ventilation,' which directly covers DLTs, OLV, and hypoxemia management including CPAP to the operative lung and PEEP to the dependent lung. Citation is valid and on-topic.

stem: A 58-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents with sudden, tearing chest pain radiating to the back. CT angiography shows an intimal flap originating in the ascending aorta and extending to the descending thoracic aorta (Stanford type A). Blood pressure is 188/104 mm Hg in the right arm and heart rate is 110 bpm. He is being prepared for emergent surgical repair. While awaiting the operating room, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic management of his hemodynamics?

choices.text: Start an intravenous beta-blocker (e.g., esmolol) to lower heart rate first, then add a vasodilator as needed for blood pressure control
choices.rationale: Correct: rate control with a beta-blocker first reduces aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt); adding a vasodilator before adequate beta-blockade would cause reflex tachycardia and worsen propagation.

choices.text: Start a sodium nitroprusside infusion alone to rapidly lower systolic blood pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a pure arterial vasodilator without prior beta-blockade triggers reflex tachycardia, increasing dP/dt and aortic wall stress, which can extend the dissection.

choices.text: Administer a fluid bolus and permissive hypertension to maintain end-organ perfusion
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hypertension and increased dP/dt are precisely the forces driving dissection propagation and rupture, so they must be reduced, not maintained.

choices.text: Begin a hydralazine infusion for smooth, gradual blood pressure reduction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hydralazine is a direct arteriolar dilator that produces unpredictable reflex tachycardia and is not recommended as primary therapy in acute aortic dissection.

choices.text: Give intravenous nicardipine targeting heart rate reduction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: nicardipine is a dihydropyridine that lowers blood pressure but does not reduce heart rate (and may reflexively increase it), so it does not control the shear-stress component first.

explanation: In acute aortic dissection the immediate goals are to reduce both the heart rate and the rate of rise of aortic pressure (dP/dt), which together determine aortic wall shear stress and the tendency for the dissection to propagate. Beta-blockade (commonly esmolol for its titratability) is initiated first to control heart rate and contractility; a vasodilator such as nitroprusside is added afterward only if blood pressure remains high. Starting a vasodilator before adequate beta-blockade produces reflex tachycardia that increases dP/dt and is potentially catastrophic. A Stanford type A dissection involving the ascending aorta is a surgical emergency.
subtopic: Vascular: acute aortic dissection hemodynamic management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (IV beta-blocker first to control HR/contractility, then add a vasodilator for residual hypertension) is the single best answer and matches the 2022 ACC/AHA aortic disease guideline, where IV beta-blockade is a Class I first-line agent and vasodilators (nitroprusside, nicardipine) are adjuncts started only after beta-blockade. The dP/dt / aortic wall shear-stress rationale is accurate. All distractors are genuinely inferior: nitroprusside alone and hydralazine both cause reflex tachycardia (correctly rejected); permissive hypertension/fluid bolus drives dissection propagation (correctly rejected); and nicardipine, a dihydropyridine, does not lower HR and may reflexively raise it, so it fails to address the rate/shear component first. Esmolol is appropriately chosen for titratability. The stem does not state a numeric HR target, so the StatPearls 'HR ~60' vs the broadened 2022 guideline 'HR 60-80' nuance does not affect correctness or create ambiguity. Stanford type A correctly identified as a surgical emergency. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441963/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Aortic Dissection' chapter, which is directly on-topic and explicitly states beta-blockers (esmolol/labetalol) are first-line, nitroprusside is added if more BP control is needed, the HR goal, and that type A is a surgical emergency. Both clinical content and citation are sound.

stem: A 72-year-old woman undergoes elective open repair of a 6.0 cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm under general anesthesia. She has hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and a BMI of 31. The procedure lasts 4 hours with an estimated blood loss of 900 mL, and she is expected to have limited mobility for several days postoperatively. Her platelet count and coagulation studies are normal, and hemostasis at closure is good. Regarding venous thromboembolism prophylaxis, which strategy is most appropriate for this patient?

choices.text: Mechanical prophylaxis with intermittent pneumatic compression alone, never adding pharmacologic prophylaxis
choices.rationale: Incorrect: mechanical prophylaxis is appropriate when bleeding risk is high, but this high-VTE-risk patient should transition to combined or pharmacologic prophylaxis once hemostasis is secure.

choices.text: Combined mechanical prophylaxis plus pharmacologic prophylaxis (low-molecular-weight or unfractionated heparin) once surgical hemostasis is confirmed
choices.rationale: Correct: this major vascular surgery patient with multiple risk factors and prolonged immobility is high risk for VTE, so combined mechanical and pharmacologic prophylaxis (after adequate hemostasis) is indicated.

choices.text: No prophylaxis because early ambulation alone is sufficient after vascular surgery
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a high-risk surgical patient with prolonged anticipated immobility requires active prophylaxis, not ambulation alone.

choices.text: Full therapeutic-dose anticoagulation with a heparin infusion for the first 48 hours
choices.rationale: Incorrect: therapeutic anticoagulation is treatment dosing, not prophylaxis, and unnecessarily increases bleeding risk in a fresh postoperative aortic patient.

choices.text: A prophylactic inferior vena cava filter placed at the time of surgery
choices.rationale: Incorrect: IVC filters are reserved for patients with VTE who cannot be anticoagulated, not as routine primary prophylaxis in a patient who can receive pharmacologic agents.

explanation: This patient has multiple VTE risk factors (major vascular surgery, age, obesity, diabetes, prolonged operative time, and anticipated immobility), placing her in a high-risk category by Caprini-type stratification. Guideline-based prophylaxis for high-risk surgical patients combines mechanical methods (intermittent pneumatic compression) with pharmacologic agents such as low-molecular-weight heparin or low-dose unfractionated heparin, started once surgical hemostasis is assured. Mechanical prophylaxis alone is appropriate only when bleeding risk remains prohibitive, and therapeutic anticoagulation or routine IVC filters are not appropriate primary prophylaxis.
subtopic: Vascular: perioperative venous thromboembolism prophylaxis
reference_title: Deep Venous Thrombosis Prophylaxis (StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (B, combined mechanical + pharmacologic prophylaxis with LMWH/LDUH once hemostasis is confirmed) is the single best answer for this high-VTE-risk patient (major open AAA repair, age 72, BMI 31, diabetes, prolonged operative time, anticipated immobility) per CHEST/ACCP and Caprini-based stratification. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A is wrong due to the absolute 'never adding pharmacologic' (mechanical-alone is reserved for prohibitive bleeding risk and should escalate once hemostasis is secure); C (no prophylaxis/ambulation alone) is inadequate for a high-risk patient; D (therapeutic-dose heparin infusion) is treatment dosing, not prophylaxis, and adds bleeding risk in a fresh aortic patient; E (prophylactic IVC filter) is not primary prophylaxis when the patient can be anticoagulated. The 'once surgical hemostasis is confirmed' qualifier correctly resolves the post-AAA bleeding concern, making B unambiguously superior to A. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534865/ resolves to StatPearls 'Deep Venous Thrombosis Prophylaxis,' which directly covers mechanical (IPC) and pharmacologic (LMWH, UFH) prophylaxis, risk stratification, and surgical patients — fully on-topic. Both clinical content and citation are sound.

stem: A 71-year-old man is undergoing transurethral resection of the prostate under spinal anesthesia. He takes phenelzine for treatment-resistant depression, which was not discontinued preoperatively. Twenty minutes after a T8 block, his blood pressure falls to 78/44 mmHg and heart rate is 52/min. You decide to treat the hypotension pharmacologically. Which agent is the MOST appropriate first-line vasopressor in this patient?

choices.text: Ephedrine 10 mg IV
choices.rationale: Ephedrine acts largely by indirectly releasing endogenous norepinephrine, which can precipitate a hypertensive crisis in a patient taking a monoamine oxidase inhibitor.

choices.text: Metaraminol 0.5 mg IV
choices.rationale: Metaraminol has substantial indirect (norepinephrine-releasing) activity and shares the same exaggerated pressor risk with MAO inhibitors as ephedrine.

choices.text: Tyramine-containing sympathomimetic
choices.rationale: Tyramine is the classic precipitant of the MAOI 'cheese reaction' and is never a therapeutic vasopressor choice.

choices.text: Phenylephrine 50-100 mcg IV
choices.rationale: Phenylephrine is a direct-acting alpha-1 agonist whose effect does not depend on norepinephrine release, making it the safest choice and additionally appropriate for the bradycardia-associated hypotension of a high sympathetic block.

choices.text: Cocaine-based topical vasoconstrictor
choices.rationale: Cocaine blocks norepinephrine reuptake and is contraindicated with MAO inhibitors, besides being inappropriate as a systemic vasopressor.

explanation: Indirect-acting sympathomimetics (ephedrine, metaraminol) work by displacing stored norepinephrine from nerve terminals. In a patient on a monoamine oxidase inhibitor, presynaptic norepinephrine stores are markedly increased because the enzyme that degrades them is inhibited, so an indirect agent can trigger a severe, unpredictable hypertensive response. A direct-acting agent such as phenylephrine bypasses this mechanism by stimulating alpha-1 receptors itself; it also reflexively slows the heart, which is acceptable here, and corrects the decreased afterload of a sympathectomy from neuraxial block.
subtopic: Vasopressors — indirect sympathomimetics and MAO inhibitor interaction

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Phenylephrine (keyed) is the textbook-correct choice for an MAOI patient because its direct alpha-1 action does not depend on norepinephrine release, avoiding the augmented presynaptic NE stores that make indirect agents (ephedrine) dangerous — the classic relative contraindication of indirect sympathomimetics with MAOIs. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: ephedrine (indirect → hypertensive crisis risk), tyramine (cheese-reaction precipitant, not a vasopressor), and cocaine (NET blocker, contraindicated and not a systemic pressor) are unambiguously wrong. Metaraminol's rationale slightly overstates its indirect component (it is predominantly direct-acting with a partial indirect/false-transmitter mechanism), but classic anesthesia teaching does caution metaraminol use with MAOIs, and even if considered it is inferior to pure-direct phenylephrine, so the single-best key is not threatened. The only physiologic wrinkle is the concurrent bradycardia (HR 52): phenylephrine causes reflex bradycardia, which the explanation acknowledges as acceptable. This does not create a second defensible answer among the offered choices, since every alternative is contraindicated or inappropriate in an MAOI patient. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK547661/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Ephedrine' article, which directly substantiates the central teaching point — it explicitly states ephedrine acts primarily as an indirect sympathomimetic and should be avoided within 14 days of MAOI therapy due to excessive synaptic norepinephrine causing hypertensive crisis. On-topic and valid for a question titled 'Ephedrine.'

stem: Forty minutes into a sevoflurane-based general anesthetic for elective shoulder arthroscopy in a healthy 24-year-old, you note an unexplained rise in end-tidal CO2 to 68 mmHg despite increased minute ventilation, masseter and generalized muscle rigidity, tachycardia of 138/min, and a temperature climbing from 37.2 to 39.1 C. You stop the volatile agent, call for help and the malignant hyperthermia cart, and convert to a non-triggering technique. Regarding dantrolene, which statement is correct and should guide your immediate management?

choices.text: The initial dose is 2.5 mg/kg IV, repeated every 5-10 minutes until signs resolve, with no fixed maximum if the crisis continues
choices.rationale: This is the correct evidence-based regimen: 2.5 mg/kg boluses titrated to physiologic response (falling ETCO2, resolving rigidity, normalizing temperature/heart rate), and the dose is not capped while the crisis persists.

choices.text: Dantrolene should be withheld until the serum creatine kinase confirms rhabdomyolysis
choices.rationale: Treatment is based on clinical recognition; waiting for laboratory confirmation delays therapy and increases mortality.

choices.text: Reconstituting each 20 mg vial of conventional dantrolene is fast because it dissolves instantly in saline
choices.rationale: Conventional dantrolene is notoriously slow to reconstitute (each 20 mg vial needs 60 mL of sterile water and vigorous shaking), which is why multiple personnel are needed.

choices.text: Dantrolene works by blocking acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction
choices.rationale: Dantrolene acts intracellularly on the ryanodine receptor (RyR1) to reduce calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, not at the neuromuscular junction.

choices.text: Calcium channel blockers such as verapamil should be co-administered with dantrolene to control the tachycardia
choices.rationale: Combining dantrolene with verapamil can precipitate hyperkalemia and cardiovascular collapse and is specifically contraindicated.

explanation: Dantrolene is the only specific pharmacologic treatment for malignant hyperthermia; it antagonizes the defective skeletal-muscle ryanodine receptor (RyR1), reducing pathologic sarcoplasmic calcium release and halting the hypermetabolic cascade. The initial dose is 2.5 mg/kg IV given as a rapid bolus and repeated as needed every few minutes, titrated to resolution of hypercarbia, rigidity, tachycardia, and rising temperature, without a hard ceiling while signs persist. Treatment is initiated on clinical suspicion alone, and calcium channel blockers are avoided because their combination with dantrolene risks lethal hyperkalemia.
subtopic: Malignant hyperthermia — dantrolene mechanism and dosing

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (choice 0) is unambiguously the single best statement. The dantrolene regimen — 2.5 mg/kg IV initial bolus, repeated every few minutes titrated to physiologic response (falling EtCO2, resolving rigidity, normalizing temp/HR), with no hard maximum while the crisis persists — is correct per MHAUS guidance and the cited reference (which also notes average effective dose ~5 mg/kg). All four distractors are genuinely false and inferior, and each rationale is accurate: (1) withholding until CK confirms rhabdo is wrong (treat on clinical suspicion); (2) conventional dantrolene is slow to reconstitute (60 mL sterile water per 20 mg vial), not instant; (3) dantrolene acts on RyR1/sarcoplasmic calcium release, not ACh at the NMJ; (4) dantrolene + verapamil risks hyperkalemia/cardiovascular collapse and is contraindicated. The stem is a textbook fulminant MH presentation with appropriate initial management already described, and the explanation is accurate and current. No ambiguity. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/malignant-hyperthermia/ resolves successfully and is directly on-topic, explicitly covering MH pathophysiology, RYR1 mechanism, the 2.5 mg/kg initial dantrolene dose, and the 60 mL/20 mg vial reconstitution detail — matching the question content precisely.

stem: An 82-year-old, 60-kg woman with chronic kidney disease is having an ultrasound-guided interscalene block with 30 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine for shoulder surgery. Several minutes after injection she becomes agitated and reports perioral numbness and tinnitus, then has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure followed by a wide-complex bradycardia and loss of pulse. The seizure is controlled and CPR is begun. In addition to high-quality resuscitation and airway management, which intervention is MOST specific to the underlying toxicity?

choices.text: Administer high-dose epinephrine boluses of 1 mg IV per ACLS for cardiac arrest
choices.rationale: In local anesthetic systemic toxicity, epinephrine should be reduced to small boluses (<= 1 mcg/kg) because large doses can worsen arrhythmias and impair resuscitation.

choices.text: Give a calcium chloride bolus to stabilize the myocardium
choices.rationale: Calcium does not reverse the sodium-channel blockade responsible for bupivacaine cardiotoxicity and is not part of LAST treatment.

choices.text: Begin 20% lipid emulsion: 1.5 mL/kg bolus over 1 minute, then an infusion at 0.25 mL/kg/min
choices.rationale: Intravenous lipid emulsion is the specific antidote for LAST, creating a 'lipid sink' and improving cardiac energetics, with the ASRA-recommended bolus of 1.5 mL/kg of 20% emulsion followed by an infusion.

choices.text: Administer flumazenil to reverse the seizure and restore consciousness
choices.rationale: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines and has no role in local anesthetic toxicity, where it could lower the seizure threshold.

choices.text: Give amiodarone 300 mg IV to treat the wide-complex rhythm
choices.rationale: Amiodarone is itself a sodium-channel blocker and may compound bupivacaine cardiotoxicity; ASRA advises avoiding local-anesthetic-like antiarrhythmics in LAST.

explanation: This is local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) from bupivacaine, which has high cardiac affinity and produces refractory sodium-channel blockade and arrest. The specific antidote is 20% intravenous lipid emulsion, dosed per the ASRA checklist as a 1.5 mL/kg bolus over about 1 minute followed by an infusion of 0.25 mL/kg/min, with bolus repetition and infusion up-titration for persistent instability. Standard ACLS is modified in LAST: epinephrine is given in small doses, and sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmics such as amiodarone, lidocaine, and procainamide are avoided.
subtopic: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity — lipid emulsion rescue
reference_title: Checklist for Treatment of Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (ASRA, 2020)

verdict.reason: REFERENCE: The reference_url resolves and is the genuine ASRA "Checklist for Treatment of Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (2020)" page — directly on-topic. CLINICAL: The keyed answer (C) is the single best answer. 20% lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg bolus over 1 min then 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion is the exact ASRA 2020 dosing and the specific antidote for LAST. The stem is a classic, unambiguous LAST presentation (bupivacaine, perioral numbness/tinnitus, seizure, cardiac arrest). Distractors are genuinely inferior: full 1 mg epinephrine boluses are contraindicated (ASRA: reduce to <=1 mcg/kg); calcium does not reverse Na-channel blockade; flumazenil is irrelevant and lowers seizure threshold; amiodarone is not the most-specific intervention. One MINOR inaccuracy: the rationale for choice E mischaracterizes amiodarone as a sodium-channel blocker to be avoided — ASRA actually lists amiodarone as the PREFERRED antiarrhythmic if one is needed in LAST (it is a class III agent), while lidocaine/procainamide are the Na-channel blockers to avoid. The explanation text also lumps amiodarone in with lidocaine/procainamide as agents to avoid, which is imprecise. However, this does not change the key, create answer ambiguity, or make any distractor competitive — lipid emulsion remains unambiguously the best answer. Net: clinically sound and reference valid; keep with a minor note that the amiodarone distractor rationale should be corrected to reflect that amiodarone is acceptable/preferred (not avoided) in LAST.

stem: A 58-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents for excision of a recurrent supraglottic squamous cell carcinoma. He reports a 2-month history of progressive hoarseness and recent-onset stridor when supine. Examination shows a mouth opening of 4 cm, Mallampati class II, full neck range of motion, and a thyromental distance of 7 cm. Prior office nasendoscopy by the surgeon documented a bulky friable supraglottic mass partially obscuring the laryngeal inlet. Vital signs are normal and oxygen saturation is 96% on room air. Which airway management plan is most appropriate?

choices.text: Standard IV rapid-sequence induction with rocuronium followed by direct laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Inducing apnea and paralysis in a patient with a friable obstructing supraglottic mass risks a 'cannot intubate, cannot ventilate' scenario with no escape, because the obstruction is at the larynx itself.

choices.text: Inhalational induction with sevoflurane maintaining spontaneous ventilation, then direct laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Although spontaneous ventilation is preserved, deepening anesthesia can cause loss of pharyngeal tone and complete obstruction by the supraglottic mass, and reassuming an awake state is slow.

choices.text: IV induction with propofol and a planned awake tracheostomy only if intubation fails
choices.rationale: Attempting asleep intubation first and reserving surgical airway as rescue inverts the safe sequence, exposing the patient to obstruction during a period when front-of-neck access has not been pre-secured.

choices.text: Awake fiberoptic or videolaryngoscopic intubation under topical anesthesia with the surgeon and tracheostomy equipment immediately available
choices.rationale: Keeping the patient awake preserves spontaneous ventilation, airway tone, and protective reflexes while the airway is secured, and immediate surgical backup provides a front-of-neck rescue if the inlet cannot be passed.

choices.text: Awake placement of a second-generation supraglottic airway device
choices.rationale: A supraglottic airway seats at the level of the pathology and cannot bypass or stent open a supraglottic obstructing lesion, so it does not secure this airway.

explanation: This patient has clear predictors of a difficult airway from pathology rather than anatomy: a known friable obstructing supraglottic mass with positional stridor, signaling impending dynamic obstruction. The ASA difficult airway algorithm directs entry into the awake-intubation pathway when difficult intubation is anticipated together with risk of obstruction or rapid desaturation, because maintaining spontaneous ventilation and consciousness preserves an escape route. Critically, induction of anesthesia and paralysis can convert a partial obstruction into a complete one with no ability to mask-ventilate, so an awake technique with immediate surgical-airway backup is the safest plan.
subtopic: Anticipated difficult airway — awake intubation decision-making

verdict.reason: Clinical content is sound and the key (D) is unambiguously best. This is the textbook anticipated-difficult-airway-from-pathology scenario: a friable, bulky, obstructing supraglottic SCC with positional stridor. The danger is converting partial to complete obstruction on induction/paralysis with inability to mask-ventilate or intubate, and the lesion is at the larynx so an SGA cannot bypass it. Awake fiberoptic/videolaryngoscopic intubation under topical with the surgeon and tracheostomy equipment immediately available correctly preserves spontaneous ventilation, tone, and reflexes with a pre-prepared front-of-neck rescue — aligned with the ASA difficult airway algorithm's awake pathway. All distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: A (RSI/paralysis) removes the escape route; B (inhalational, spontaneous vent) still risks loss of tone/complete obstruction and slow emergence with a friable mass; C inverts the safe sequence (reactive rather than pre-secured FONA); E (SGA) seats at the level of pathology and cannot stent the lesion. The reassuring anatomic exam (MP II, 4 cm mouth opening, full neck ROM, TMD 7 cm) is a deliberate distractor reinforcing that difficulty is pathology-driven. The only theoretical alternative — awake tracheostomy-first under local — is not offered as a clean choice, so D is not ambiguous. The explanation is accurate and current. Reference (StatPearls 'Difficult Airway', NBK470224) resolves via WebFetch and is directly on-topic, covering anticipated difficult airway, awake fiberoptic/videolaryngoscopic intubation, and airway obstruction.

stem: A 44-year-old woman undergoes an uncomplicated total thyroidectomy for multinodular goiter and is extubated awake in the operating room. Four hours later in the PACU she develops increasing neck swelling, complains that her dressing feels tight, and reports difficulty breathing. She is sitting upright, anxious, and has inspiratory stridor; the surgical site is tensely swollen and the trachea is difficult to palpate. Oxygen saturation has fallen from 98% to 90% on 4 L/min nasal cannula. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.text: Administer racemic epinephrine nebulizer and dexamethasone, then observe
choices.rationale: Nebulized epinephrine and steroids treat mucosal edema but will not relieve mechanical extrinsic compression from an expanding hematoma, and observation wastes time during a rapidly progressing airway emergency.

choices.text: Open the surgical incision at the bedside to evacuate the hematoma and decompress the airway
choices.rationale: Releasing the clips/sutures to evacuate the hematoma directly relieves the extrinsic compression and venous congestion that are obstructing the airway, and is the recommended immediate maneuver before or alongside reintubation.

choices.text: Perform awake fiberoptic intubation in the PACU before addressing the wound
choices.rationale: Distorted, edematous anatomy from the hematoma makes fiberoptic intubation difficult and slow, and securing a tube does not address the ongoing compression that is the root cause.

choices.text: Take the patient emergently for CT of the neck to define the hematoma
choices.rationale: Transporting an obstructing-airway patient for imaging delays definitive decompression and risks complete obstruction away from rescue equipment.

choices.text: Reassure the patient and elevate the head of the bed while awaiting the surgeon
choices.rationale: Passive positioning does nothing to relieve a tense expanding hematoma, and a falling saturation with stridor mandates immediate active decompression rather than watchful waiting.

explanation: Post-thyroidectomy cervical hematoma is a time-critical airway emergency, typically within the first 6 hours, that compresses the trachea and obstructs venous and lymphatic drainage, producing laryngeal edema and stridor. The lifesaving first step is to open the wound at the bedside to evacuate the clot and relieve compression; this often dramatically improves the airway and may be required before an endotracheal tube can even be passed. This is distinct from recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, which causes hoarseness or biphasic stridor without neck swelling and is not relieved by opening the wound.
subtopic: ENT surgical emergency — post-thyroidectomy neck hematoma

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 1 — open the incision at the bedside to evacuate the hematoma) is the single best, lifesaving first maneuver for an expanding post-thyroidectomy cervical hematoma with airway compromise. This is standard ENT/anesthesia board teaching: decompress the wound immediately, before or alongside reintubation, because relieving extrinsic compression and venous/lymphatic congestion often dramatically improves the airway and may be required before a tube can be passed. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: racemic epi/steroids treat only mucosal edema and pair with dangerous observation; awake fiberoptic addresses neither the root cause nor the distorted anatomy and is slow; CT delays definitive care and transports an obstructing patient; reassurance/positioning is passive in a progressing emergency. Rationales and the explanation are accurate and current, including the correct pathophysiology, the first-6-hours time window (fits the 4-hour presentation), and the accurate contrast with RLN injury. The 'most appropriate immediate intervention' phrasing makes the answer unambiguous; the reintubation nuance is explicitly accommodated by the keyed answer ('before or alongside reintubation'). REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563279/ resolves to 'Thyroidectomy - StatPearls', directly on-topic, and explicitly supports the key, stating the wound 'may need to be reopened immediately at the bedside, with the potential for an emergency tracheostomy to secure the airway.' Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 24-year-old man presents for mediastinoscopy and biopsy of a newly discovered large anterior mediastinal mass suspicious for lymphoma. He reports a dry cough and dyspnea that worsens when lying flat, and facial fullness in the mornings. CT shows a bulky mass compressing the distal trachea and right main bronchus with an estimated 60% reduction in tracheal cross-sectional area, plus partial compression of the superior vena cava. He is comfortable sitting upright with a saturation of 95% on room air. Which feature most strongly predicts life-threatening cardiorespiratory collapse on induction of general anesthesia and should most alter the anesthetic plan?

choices.text: Greater than 50% compression of the tracheobronchial tree with postural (supine) symptoms
choices.rationale: Severe airway compression combined with positional symptoms identifies a dynamic obstruction that worsens with loss of muscle tone, spontaneous ventilation, and the supine position, the hallmark high-risk profile for collapse on induction.

choices.text: Presence of a dry cough
choices.rationale: A nonproductive cough is a common, nonspecific symptom of mediastinal masses and does not by itself stratify the risk of induction-related collapse.

choices.text: A room-air oxygen saturation of 95% while seated
choices.rationale: A near-normal resting saturation in the upright position is reassuring but does not capture the dynamic deterioration that occurs once the patient is supine and anesthetized.

choices.text: The patient's young age of 24 years
choices.rationale: Age alone is not a predictor of airway or cardiovascular collapse; the mechanical and positional features of the mass drive the risk.

choices.text: A presumptive tissue diagnosis of lymphoma
choices.rationale: Although lymphoma may shrink with pre-biopsy steroids or radiation, the histologic suspicion itself does not quantify the immediate mechanical risk of anesthesia.

explanation: In anterior mediastinal mass patients, the most dangerous scenario is dynamic compression of the airway and great vessels that is unmasked by general anesthesia: muscle relaxation, loss of spontaneous negative-pressure ventilation, and supine positioning all reduce the transmural pressure that holds the compressed airway open, and compression of the heart and pulmonary artery can cause cardiovascular collapse. High-risk features include greater than 50% tracheobronchial compression, postural (especially supine) symptoms, stridor, pericardial effusion, and SVC syndrome. These findings favor preserving spontaneous ventilation, avoiding neuromuscular blockade, having rigid bronchoscopy available, and considering a less invasive diagnostic approach or pretreatment before committing to general anesthesia.
subtopic: Shared/dynamic airway — anterior mediastinal mass collapse

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (>50% tracheobronchial compression combined with postural/supine symptoms) is the single best answer and is corroborated by current literature (APSF, CJA Continuing Professional Development) as the dominant high-risk profile predicting cardiorespiratory collapse on induction. The mechanism in the explanation — loss of muscle tone, loss of spontaneous negative-pressure ventilation, and supine positioning reducing the transmural pressure that splints the compressed airway, plus great-vessel/PA compression — is accurate and current. All distractors are genuinely inferior: dry cough is nonspecific; upright SpO2 95% is reassuring but not predictive of dynamic supine deterioration; age 24 is not a predictor; lymphoma histology does not quantify mechanical risk. The stem gives a patient with BOTH keyed features (60% tracheal/RMB compression + orthopnea/positional dyspnea + partial SVC compression), so the answer is unambiguous. The compound nature of the correct option is defensible because it is precisely the recognized highest-risk combination and no second option is defensible. REFERENCE: WebFetch confirms the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves to 'Mediastinal Masses: Adults, Mediastinoscopy' (updated Nov 2023), which directly covers anterior mediastinal mass risk factors, airway/vascular compression, positional symptoms, spontaneous-ventilation management, and mediastinoscopy. Title and topic match the citation. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 44-year-old woman with episodic headaches, palpitations, and diaphoresis is scheduled for laparoscopic resection of a 5-cm right adrenal mass after biochemical workup confirmed elevated plasma metanephrines. She was started on phenoxybenzamine 12 days ago, and a beta-blocker was added 4 days ago; her resting blood pressure is now 118/74 mmHg with a heart rate of 88/min and she reports mild nasal congestion and lightheadedness on standing. During surgical manipulation of the tumor, her blood pressure abruptly rises to 245/140 mmHg with a heart rate of 130/min. Which agent is the most appropriate first-line intervention for this hypertensive crisis?

choices.text: Phenylephrine infusion
choices.rationale: Phenylephrine is an alpha-agonist vasopressor that would worsen the catecholamine-driven vasoconstriction and further elevate blood pressure.

choices.text: Esmolol bolus
choices.rationale: A pure beta-blocker given for an acute pressor surge leaves alpha-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed and can paradoxically worsen hypertension and precipitate heart failure.

choices.text: Sodium nitroprusside infusion
choices.rationale: Nitroprusside is a rapidly titratable direct arterial and venous dilator that is ideally suited to control the short-lived, severe hypertensive surges that occur with tumor manipulation.

choices.text: Intravenous fluid bolus alone
choices.rationale: Volume expansion addresses the post-resection hypotension risk but does nothing to control an acute intraoperative catecholamine-mediated hypertensive crisis.

choices.text: Vasopressin bolus
choices.rationale: Vasopressin is a vasoconstrictor used for refractory hypotension and would dangerously compound the hypertensive crisis.

explanation: During pheochromocytoma resection, tumor manipulation releases boluses of catecholamines that cause abrupt, transient, severe hypertension. The ideal agent is short-acting and rapidly titratable so it dissipates once the surge resolves, avoiding overshoot hypotension; sodium nitroprusside, nicardipine, phentolamine, or clevidipine are all reasonable choices. A pure beta-blocker must never be used to treat the surge because unopposed alpha stimulation worsens vasoconstriction, and alpha-agonist or other vasopressors are contraindicated during the hypertensive phase.
subtopic: Pheochromocytoma: intraoperative hemodynamic management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Stem is a textbook pheochromocytoma resection with correct preop prep (phenoxybenzamine started 12 days before beta-blocker, signs of adequate alpha blockade) and an intraoperative catecholamine surge from tumor manipulation. The keyed answer, sodium nitroprusside, is a classic rapidly-titratable short-acting direct vasodilator ideal for these transient surges. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: phenylephrine and vasopressin are vasoconstrictors that would worsen the crisis; a pure beta-blocker (esmolol) leaves alpha-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed and is contraindicated; IV fluids do not treat acute hypertension. The explanation correctly notes that nicardipine, phentolamine, and clevidipine are also acceptable agents and that beta-blockers must never be used for the surge — but since none of those alternative correct agents appear among the choices, nitroprusside is the single best answer with no competing defensible option. Rationales are accurate and current.\n\nREFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keyword/pheochromocytoma/ resolved fully and is directly on-topic. The page explicitly states intraoperative hypertension is treated with direct-acting arteriolar vasodilators such as sodium nitroprusside and nicardipine, that tumor manipulation causes the greatest catecholamine release, and that short-acting antihypertensives should be anticipated near adrenal venous clamping — fully supporting the keyed answer.

stem: A 58-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis taking prednisone 10 mg daily for the past 8 months presents for an urgent open sigmoid colectomy for perforated diverticulitis. He is febrile to 38.6 C, tachycardic at 118/min, and hypotensive at 86/52 mmHg despite a 2-liter crystalloid bolus, with a serum sodium of 131 mEq/L and potassium of 5.2 mEq/L. After induction and through the early part of the case he remains hypotensive and poorly responsive to phenylephrine and norepinephrine. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg
choices.rationale: Chronic exogenous steroids suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, and this patient with refractory hypotension under major surgical stress requires stress-dose glucocorticoid replacement.

choices.text: Administer fludrocortisone orally
choices.rationale: Oral fludrocortisone is impractical intraoperatively and unnecessary because stress-dose hydrocortisone at high doses already provides sufficient mineralocorticoid activity.

choices.text: Withhold any steroid and continue escalating vasopressors
choices.rationale: Continuing to escalate vasopressors without replacing glucocorticoid fails to treat the underlying adrenal insufficiency and perpetuates catecholamine resistance.

choices.text: Administer dexamethasone 4 mg and order a random cortisol level before any further treatment
choices.rationale: Although dexamethasone does not interfere with a cortisol assay, delaying treatment to await a level is unsafe in a hemodynamically unstable patient who needs immediate hydrocortisone.

choices.text: Administer intravenous calcium gluconate to treat the hyperkalemia as the cause of hypotension
choices.rationale: A potassium of 5.2 mEq/L is mildly elevated and is a manifestation of adrenal insufficiency rather than the cause of refractory hypotension.

explanation: Patients on chronic glucocorticoids (roughly greater than 5 mg prednisone daily for more than 3 weeks) have a suppressed HPA axis and cannot mount an adequate cortisol response to surgical stress, producing perioperative adrenal crisis with vasopressor-refractory hypotension, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia. The immediate treatment is empiric stress-dose hydrocortisone (commonly 100 mg IV followed by 50 mg every 8 hours or a continuous infusion for major surgery); high-dose hydrocortisone provides intrinsic mineralocorticoid effect, so separate fludrocortisone is not needed acutely. Treatment should not be delayed to obtain confirmatory cortisol testing in an unstable patient.
subtopic: Adrenal insufficiency: perioperative stress-dose steroid coverage

verdict.reason: Clinically sound and unambiguous. The stem describes a classic perioperative adrenal crisis: prednisone 10 mg/day x 8 months (well above the ~5 mg x 3 weeks HPA-suppression threshold) undergoing major emergency surgery with vasopressor-refractory hypotension, hyponatremia (Na 131), and mild hyperkalemia (K 5.2). Empiric IV hydrocortisone 100 mg (Choice A) is the correct, guideline-concordant immediate treatment. All distractors are genuinely inferior: fludrocortisone is impractical and redundant since high-dose hydrocortisone provides mineralocorticoid activity (B); withholding steroid and escalating pressors fails to treat the cause (C); dexamethasone + awaiting a random cortisol unsafely delays treatment in an unstable patient (D, with an accurate teaching point that dexamethasone does not interfere with the cortisol assay); and K 5.2 is a manifestation, not the cause, of hypotension so calcium gluconate is wrong (E). The explanation's dosing, pathophysiology, and threshold are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK279083/ resolves to a comprehensive, on-topic review of adrenal insufficiency covering chronic-steroid causes, hypotension/hyponatremia/hyperkalemia, adrenal crisis, and perioperative stress-dose hydrocortisone (e.g., 'HC 100mg IV just before induction... 200mg HC in 24h'). Minor cosmetic note: the page is actually Endotext, not StatPearls as the reference_title states, but the NBK ID is correct, resolves, and is fully on-topic, so this is not a citation problem.

stem: A 31-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes (most recent HbA1c 6.8%) at 39 weeks gestation is admitted in active labor and managed with a continuous insulin infusion and dextrose-containing fluid per protocol, maintaining intrapartum glucose between 80 and 110 mg/dL. She delivers a 4.1-kg infant. Forty-five minutes after delivery of the placenta, while the insulin infusion continues unchanged, the mother's fingerstick glucose falls to 48 mg/dL and the neonate develops jitteriness with a heel-stick glucose of 32 mg/dL. Which physiologic change best explains the abrupt hypoglycemia seen in both mother and neonate?

choices.text: Delivery of the placenta abruptly removes placental anti-insulin hormones, causing a sharp drop in maternal insulin requirements while fetal hyperinsulinemia persists transiently
choices.rationale: Loss of placental human placental lactogen and other counterregulatory hormones at delivery sharply lowers maternal insulin needs, and the neonate's pancreas, having adapted to chronic maternal hyperglycemia, continues to oversecrete insulin and becomes hypoglycemic.

choices.text: Oxytocin administered after delivery has a direct insulin-like hypoglycemic effect on both mother and neonate
choices.rationale: Oxytocin does not have a clinically significant insulin-like glucose-lowering action that would explain hypoglycemia in both patients.

choices.text: Maternal catecholamine surge during the second stage of labor suppresses neonatal insulin secretion
choices.rationale: Labor catecholamines tend to raise rather than lower glucose, and this mechanism does not explain persistent neonatal hyperinsulinemia after delivery.

choices.text: Breastfeeding within the first hour transfers maternal insulin to the neonate through colostrum
choices.rationale: Insulin is not transferred in clinically meaningful amounts through colostrum, and this would not explain the simultaneous maternal hypoglycemia.

choices.text: The neonate's immature renal tubules waste glucose, producing hypoglycemia independent of insulin
choices.rationale: Neonatal hypoglycemia of the infant of a diabetic mother is driven by hyperinsulinemia, not renal glucosuria.

explanation: Insulin resistance in pregnancy is driven largely by placental hormones such as human placental lactogen, and delivery of the placenta removes them almost immediately, causing maternal insulin requirements to plummet; insulin infusions must therefore be sharply reduced or stopped after delivery to avoid maternal hypoglycemia. The neonate of a mother with diabetes has been exposed to chronic transplacental glucose and develops compensatory islet hyperplasia and fetal hyperinsulinemia, so when the maternal glucose supply is cut at cord clamping the neonate becomes hypoglycemic. Tight intrapartum maternal glucose control reduces, but does not eliminate, this neonatal risk.
subtopic: Diabetes in pregnancy: peripartum glucose physiology and neonatal hypoglycemia

verdict.reason: Clinical content is correct and unambiguous. The keyed answer (A) is the single best explanation: delivery of the placenta abruptly withdraws anti-insulin hormones (HPL/human chorionic somatomammotropin), causing maternal insulin requirements to plummet so a continued unchanged infusion produces maternal hypoglycemia, while the IDM has chronic islet hyperplasia/fetal hyperinsulinemia that persists after the maternal glucose supply is cut at cord clamping, producing neonatal hypoglycemia. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and the rationales are accurate: oxytocin has no clinically meaningful insulin-like glucose-lowering effect (B); labor catecholamines raise rather than lower glucose and do not explain persistent neonatal hyperinsulinemia (C); insulin is not transferred in meaningful amounts via colostrum and that would not explain maternal hypoglycemia (D); and IDM hypoglycemia is hyperinsulinemic, not glucosuric (E). The explanation correctly notes insulin infusions must be sharply reduced/stopped post-delivery and that tight intrapartum control reduces but does not eliminate neonatal risk. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK279010/ resolves to the Endotext 'Diabetes In Pregnancy' chapter and directly corroborates the keyed mechanism, including that postpartum insulin requirements 'drop dramatically' and that the infant 'may continue to produce excessive insulin for up to 48 hours after birth.' Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 79-year-old woman with chronic atrial fibrillation presents for cataract extraction under monitored anesthesia care. She takes apixaban, which has not been held, and her cardiologist advises against interruption because of a prior embolic stroke. She also has a known axial length of 27 mm with a posterior staphyloma in the operative eye and is unable to lie completely flat without coughing. The ophthalmologist requests a regional block that will provide adequate akinesia and analgesia for the planned procedure. Which anesthetic technique is the most appropriate choice for this patient?

choices.text: Sub-Tenon's block with a blunt cannula through a conjunctival incision
choices.rationale: Correct: the blunt-cannula sub-Tenon's technique avoids a sharp needle, carries the lowest risk of globe perforation and orbital hemorrhage, and is the preferred block in anticoagulated patients and in eyes with long axial length or staphyloma.

choices.text: Retrobulbar block with a sharp needle placed into the intraconal space
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the sharp intraconal needle carries the highest risk of globe perforation and retrobulbar hemorrhage, which is especially dangerous in an anticoagulated patient with a long, staphylomatous globe.

choices.text: Peribulbar block with a sharp needle placed into the extraconal space
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although extraconal placement is somewhat safer than retrobulbar, it still uses a sharp needle that can perforate an elongated globe and cause hemorrhage in an anticoagulated patient, making it inferior to a blunt-cannula technique here.

choices.text: General anesthesia with endotracheal intubation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: general anesthesia adds cardiopulmonary and airway risk that is unnecessary for routine cataract surgery and does not address the question of which regional block is most appropriate.

choices.text: Defer surgery until the apixaban has been held for at least 5 days
choices.rationale: Incorrect: interrupting anticoagulation is not required because a low-risk technique exists, and stopping apixaban against cardiology advice exposes this prior-stroke patient to avoidable thromboembolic harm.

explanation: Choice of ophthalmic regional technique is driven by needle-related risk: globe perforation and orbital (retrobulbar) hemorrhage. Sharp-needle retrobulbar and peribulbar blocks are relatively contraindicated in eyes with long axial length or posterior staphyloma and in anticoagulated patients because of these risks. The sub-Tenon's block uses a blunt cannula introduced beneath Tenon's capsule, essentially eliminating perforation risk and minimizing bleeding, which makes it the preferred block for anticoagulated patients and high-myopia/staphylomatous eyes, and it provides reliable akinesia and analgesia for cataract surgery without interrupting therapeutic anticoagulation.
subtopic: Regional ophthalmic anesthesia: block selection in the anticoagulated, high-myopia patient (sub-Tenon's block)
reference_title: Regional Anesthesia for Ophthalmic Surgery - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (sub-Tenon's block with blunt cannula) is the single best choice. The stem stacks three convergent risk factors — therapeutic anticoagulation (apixaban not held, cardiology advises against interruption after prior embolic stroke), long axial length (27 mm) with posterior staphyloma, and inability to lie flat — all of which favor a blunt-cannula technique. Sub-Tenon's avoids the sharp needle, markedly reduces globe-perforation and orbital-hemorrhage risk, and is the standard preferred block in anticoagulated and high-myopia/staphylomatous eyes (axial length >26 mm is a classic relative contraindication to sharp-needle retro/peribulbar blocks). All distractors are genuinely inferior: retrobulbar (B) carries the highest perforation/hemorrhage risk; peribulbar (C) still uses a sharp needle that can perforate an elongated globe; GA+ETT (D) adds unnecessary cardiopulmonary/airway risk and does not answer 'which block'; deferring to hold apixaban (E) is inappropriate against cardiology advice when a safe block exists. Rationales are accurate. Minor: the explanation's 'essentially eliminating perforation risk' is appropriately hedged ('essentially'), and choice A's rationale is correct — sub-Tenon's does carry the lowest perforation risk among the options. The 'cannot lie flat without coughing' detail mildly tempts GA but does not create true ambiguity; sub-Tenon's is brief and remains the best answer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/regional-anesthesia-for-ophthalmic-surgery/ resolves (last updated 09/09/2025), titled 'Regional Anesthesia for Ophthalmic Surgery,' and directly covers retrobulbar/peribulbar/sub-Tenon's techniques, the long-axial-length contraindication for retrobulbar, and sub-Tenon's as ideal for anticoagulated patients. Citation is valid and on-topic.

stem: Immediately after a sharp-needle peribulbar block is placed for elective cataract surgery in a 74-year-old man, the eye becomes acutely tense and markedly proptotic, the eyelids are taut and difficult to retract, and the conjunctiva is ballooning with blood. Within two minutes the globe resists retropulsion, the pupil shows a new relative afferent defect, and the patient reports rapidly worsening vision in that eye. Tonometry reveals an intraocular pressure of 48 mmHg. Vital signs are stable. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?

choices.text: Apply firm continuous digital pressure to the globe for 15 minutes and proceed with the planned cataract surgery
choices.rationale: Incorrect: sustained external pressure further raises intraorbital pressure and compromises retinal and optic nerve perfusion, and proceeding with surgery ignores a sight-threatening compartment syndrome.

choices.text: Obtain an urgent orbital CT scan to confirm the diagnosis before any intervention
choices.rationale: Incorrect: orbital compartment syndrome from retrobulbar hemorrhage is a clinical diagnosis, and waiting for imaging dangerously delays vision-saving decompression.

choices.text: Perform immediate lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis to decompress the orbit
choices.rationale: Correct: canthotomy with cantholysis is the definitive emergent treatment that relieves the orbital compartment, lowers intraocular and intraorbital pressure, and restores optic nerve and retinal perfusion before irreversible ischemia occurs.

choices.text: Administer intravenous atropine 0.5 mg to treat a suspected oculocardiac reflex
choices.rationale: Incorrect: there is no bradycardia and the emergency is mechanical orbital ischemia, not a vagal reflex, so atropine does nothing to relieve the rising orbital pressure.

choices.text: Start a topical prostaglandin analog and recheck the intraocular pressure in one hour
choices.rationale: Incorrect: topical pressure-lowering drops act far too slowly and weakly to reverse acute orbital compartment ischemia, and a one-hour delay risks permanent vision loss.

explanation: Acute retrobulbar (orbital) hemorrhage after a sharp-needle block can produce orbital compartment syndrome: a tense proptotic globe, taut lids, markedly elevated intraocular pressure, resistance to retropulsion, a relative afferent pupillary defect, and rapid vision loss. The optic nerve and retina tolerate ischemia for only a short time, so this is a clinical, time-critical diagnosis that should not wait for imaging. The vision-saving intervention is immediate lateral canthotomy and cantholysis to decompress the orbit; pharmacologic measures and external pressure are inadequate and may worsen perfusion.
subtopic: Acute complications of ophthalmic blocks: retrobulbar hemorrhage and orbital compartment syndrome

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook retrobulbar hemorrhage with orbital compartment syndrome after a sharp-needle peribulbar block (tense proptotic globe, taut lids, resistance to retropulsion, new RAPD, vision loss, IOP 48 mmHg). The keyed answer — immediate lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis — is the single definitive, time-critical, vision-saving intervention and is the standard of care when optic nerve compromise plus IOP >=40 mmHg are present. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: (A) digital pressure + proceeding with surgery worsens perfusion and ignores a sight-threatening emergency; (B) orbital CT dangerously delays a clinical diagnosis; (D) atropine treats a vagal reflex that is absent (no bradycardia, stable vitals, mechanical/ischemic process); (E) topical prostaglandin analog is far too slow/weak (prostaglandins act over hours-days) and a 1-hour delay risks permanent loss. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current.\n\nREFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK576417/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Retrobulbar Hematoma' article, which directly covers RBH, orbital compartment syndrome, and lateral canthotomy/cantholysis as definitive treatment, explicitly recommending immediate decompression for IOP >=40 mmHg with optic nerve compromise within ~2 hours and warning against delay. The citation is correct and on-topic. Both clinical and reference are sound -> keep.

stem: A 70-year-old man with primary open-angle glaucoma and mild COPD is undergoing general anesthesia for a transurethral resection of the prostate. His home medication list includes timolol 0.5% ophthalmic drops, one drop to each eye twice daily, which the nurse instilled on schedule the morning of surgery. Shortly after induction the heart rate drifts to 44 beats/min and audible expiratory wheezing develops; the rhythm is sinus bradycardia and the blood pressure is 96/58 mmHg. Which mechanism most likely explains this presentation?

choices.text: Systemic absorption of the topical beta-blocker through the nasolacrimal and conjunctival mucosa
choices.rationale: Correct: ophthalmic timolol drains through the nasolacrimal duct and conjunctiva to reach the systemic circulation while bypassing first-pass metabolism, producing beta-blockade that causes bradycardia and bronchospasm.

choices.text: An oculocardiac reflex triggered by pressure on the globe during positioning
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the oculocardiac reflex requires globe or extraocular muscle traction or pressure, which is not part of a TURP, and it would not explain the new bronchospasm.

choices.text: A type I hypersensitivity reaction to the induction agent
choices.rationale: Incorrect: anaphylaxis characteristically produces tachycardia with hypotension and often urticaria, not isolated sinus bradycardia, making this an unlikely fit.

choices.text: Excessive systemic absorption of irrigating fluid causing TURP syndrome
choices.rationale: Incorrect: TURP syndrome from hypotonic irrigant absorption presents with hyponatremia, hypervolemia, and neurologic changes rather than bradycardia with wheezing this early in the case.

choices.text: Carbonic anhydrase inhibition producing a metabolic acidosis
choices.rationale: Incorrect: that effect belongs to carbonic anhydrase inhibitors such as acetazolamide or dorzolamide, not to timolol, and it would not acutely cause bradycardia and bronchospasm.

explanation: Topical ophthalmic medications are absorbed systemically, principally through the highly vascular nasolacrimal duct mucosa and conjunctiva, which bypasses hepatic first-pass metabolism. Timolol is a nonselective beta-antagonist, so systemically absorbed drug can produce clinically significant bradycardia and bronchospasm, particularly in patients with reactive airways or those receiving other negative chronotropes under anesthesia. Recognizing the drop as the culprit guides treatment with atropine for bradycardia and bronchodilators for bronchospasm, and informs cautious use of topical beta-blockers in patients with COPD or conduction disease.
subtopic: Systemic effects of topical ophthalmic drugs: timolol absorption causing bradycardia and bronchospasm

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Topical ophthalmic timolol (a nonselective beta-antagonist) drains via the nasolacrimal duct into the vascular nasal mucosa, bypassing hepatic first-pass metabolism and reaching systemic circulation; it is a classic cause of bradycardia plus bronchospasm, especially in COPD/reactive airways. The dyad of new sinus bradycardia AND expiratory wheezing in a patient given timolol that morning points uniquely to systemic beta-blockade. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: oculocardiac reflex requires globe/EOM traction (absent in TURP) and causes no bronchospasm; type I hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis causes tachycardia not bradycardia; TURP syndrome causes hyponatremia/hypervolemia/neuro changes, not this early dyad; carbonic anhydrase inhibition/metabolic acidosis belongs to acetazolamide/dorzolamide, not timolol. Explanation (atropine, bronchodilators, caution in COPD/conduction disease) is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545176/ resolves to the Timolol StatPearls chapter, which confirms systemic absorption of the ophthalmic solution (60-78% bioavailability), lists bronchospasm/bronchoconstriction and bradycardia as adverse effects, contraindicates use in asthma/COPD and bradycardia/heart block, and references nasolacrimal occlusion to reduce systemic absorption. Citation is on-topic and accurate.

stem: A healthy 3-year-old (15 kg) undergoes adenotonsillectomy under sevoflurane and an oral RAE tube. At the end of the case the surgeon removes the mouth gag and the child is extubated awake but still has secretions in the oropharynx. Within seconds the child develops complete inspiratory obstruction with paradoxical chest movement, tracheal tug, and no air entry despite a tight jaw-thrust mask seal. SpO2 falls from 99% to 86%.
