The CAA applies 100% oxygen with vigorous continuous positive airway pressure and firm bilateral pressure at the 'laryngospasm notch,' but obstruction persists and SpO2 is now 78% and falling, with heart rate dropping from 120 to 70/min. An IV is patent. Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

choices.text: Immediately attempt direct laryngoscopy and reintubation
choices.rationale: Forcing a laryngoscope past tightly adducted cords during active spasm is traumatic, delays oxygenation, and does not break the spasm—pharmacologic relaxation should come first.

choices.text: Administer IV atropine 0.02 mg/kg and continue CPAP only
choices.rationale: Atropine treats the accompanying vagally mediated bradycardia but does not relax the laryngeal musculature, so it will not relieve the persistent glottic closure causing the hypoxia.

choices.text: Administer IV succinylcholine (approximately 0.1–0.2 mg/kg, up to ~1 mg/kg) to break the spasm
choices.rationale: A small-to-full dose of IV succinylcholine rapidly relaxes the laryngeal muscles to relieve refractory laryngospasm, and atropine is co-administered or readily available to treat associated bradycardia.

choices.text: Give an additional propofol bolus of 3 mg/kg to deepen anesthesia
choices.rationale: A propofol 'mini-dose' (~0.5 mg/kg) can abort early spasm, but at this point of severe hypoxia and bradycardia a larger propofol bolus risks hypotension and is too slow and unreliable to relieve complete obstruction.

choices.text: Perform an emergent surgical cricothyrotomy
choices.rationale: Surgical airway is a last resort for a true 'cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate' situation and is not indicated when a rapidly effective pharmacologic remedy (succinylcholine) has not yet been tried.

explanation: Laryngospasm is a leading cause of perioperative respiratory morbidity and cardiac arrest in children, who have high vagal tone and low oxygen reserve. The stepwise response is removing the stimulus, 100% oxygen with CPAP, and firm jaw-thrust/Larson's maneuver; if spasm is refractory and desaturation with bradycardia ensues, IV succinylcholine (with atropine for bradycardia) is the definitive treatment to relax the cords and restore ventilation. When no IV is available, intramuscular succinylcholine (about 4 mg/kg) with IM atropine is the alternative.
subtopic: Pediatric — laryngospasm management on emergence

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 2, IV succinylcholine with atropine for bradycardia) is the single best answer for refractory pediatric laryngospasm progressing to hypoxia (SpO2 78%) with bradycardia (HR 70) after first-line maneuvers (100% O2, CPAP, Larson's maneuver) have failed. This matches standard pediatric anesthesia teaching and the cited OpenAnesthesia page. The succinylcholine dose range stated (0.1-0.2 mg/kg up to ~1 mg/kg) is defensible — low doses (0.1 mg/kg) break spasm while higher doses (up to ~1 mg/kg) are used for complete refractory spasm. Co-administration of atropine appropriately addresses the concern that succinylcholine can worsen vagally-mediated bradycardia. All distractors are genuinely inferior: reintubation (0) is traumatic and does not break spasm; atropine+CPAP only (1) does not relax laryngeal muscle and CPAP already failed; propofol 3 mg/kg (3) is too slow/unreliable for complete obstruction and risks hypotension in a hypoxic bradycardic child; cricothyrotomy (4) is premature before pharmacologic relaxation. The IM alternative (succinylcholine ~4 mg/kg with IM atropine when no IV) is accurate and matches the reference. Rationales are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/laryngospasm/ resolves successfully and is directly on-topic — it covers laryngospasm management in children, CPAP with 100% O2, Larson's maneuver (digital pressure behind the mandible), and succinylcholine including the 3-4 mg/kg IM dose for no IV access. The citation is valid and relevant.

stem: A 6-year-old boy with no significant past medical history undergoes elective strabismus repair under general anesthesia maintained with sevoflurane and nitrous oxide; no muscle relaxant was used. About 45 minutes into the case the CAA notes an unexplained rise in end-tidal CO2 to 68 mmHg despite increasing minute ventilation, tachycardia to 165/min, and masseter rigidity noted earlier at induction. Temperature has risen from 36.6 C to 39.1 C and the soda lime canister feels hot. An arterial gas shows a mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis with hyperkalemia. Which intervention is the single most important to reduce mortality in this crisis?

choices.text: Switch from sevoflurane to a propofol infusion and continue the procedure
choices.rationale: Discontinuing the volatile trigger and converting to a non-triggering technique is essential, but on its own it does not reverse the established hypermetabolic cascade that is killing the patient.

choices.text: Administer IV insulin with dextrose and calcium to treat the hyperkalemia
choices.rationale: Treating life-threatening hyperkalemia is a necessary supportive measure, but it addresses a consequence of the crisis rather than the underlying pathophysiology that dantrolene halts.

choices.text: Begin aggressive surface and core cooling with cold IV fluids and stop all warming
choices.rationale: Active cooling is an important adjunct for hyperthermia, but it does not stop the runaway calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum that drives the entire syndrome.

choices.text: Hyperventilate with 100% oxygen at high fresh gas flows to clear CO2
choices.rationale: Hyperventilation with high-flow oxygen helps wash out volatile agent and correct hypercarbia but is only supportive and will not terminate the hypermetabolic reaction.

choices.text: Administer IV dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg, repeated as needed, while calling for help and the MH cart
choices.rationale: Dantrolene inhibits pathologic ryanodine-receptor calcium release in skeletal muscle and is the only drug that directly reverses malignant hyperthermia, so prompt dosing (2.5 mg/kg, repeated to effect) is the intervention most associated with survival.

explanation: This is a classic intraoperative malignant hyperthermia crisis: a volatile-agent trigger, masseter rigidity, rising end-tidal CO2 refractory to ventilation, hyperthermia, tachycardia, mixed acidosis, and hyperkalemia. While stopping the trigger, hyperventilating with 100% oxygen, cooling, and treating hyperkalemia are all part of management, dantrolene is the definitive therapy because it directly antagonizes the uncontrolled sarcoplasmic calcium release of the RYR1 channel defect. Early dantrolene administration is the single intervention most strongly tied to reduced mortality, and MHAUS support should be activated.
subtopic: Pediatric — malignant hyperthermia crisis management

verdict.reason: Clinical: Textbook intraoperative MH crisis (pediatric strabismus repair, sevoflurane trigger, masseter rigidity, EtCO2 refractory to ventilation, hyperthermia, tachycardia, mixed acidosis, hyperkalemia, hot soda lime). Keyed answer (index 4, dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV repeated to effect) is unambiguously the single best answer: it is the only agent that reduces MH mortality, with correct dose and correct mechanism (RYR1/ryanodine-receptor sarcoplasmic calcium release). All four distractors (propofol switch + continue case, insulin/dextrose/calcium for hyperkalemia, active cooling, hyperventilation with 100% O2) are genuine but supportive measures and are correctly framed as inferior; none constitutes a competing best answer to a stem asking specifically what most reduces mortality. Rationales are accurate and current. Reference: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430828/ resolves to 'Malignant Hyperthermia - StatPearls' (NCBI Bookshelf), directly on-topic, and explicitly states 'Dantrolene in a dose of 2.5 mg/kg must be administered intravenously as soon as possible, up to a maximum dose of 10 mg/kg' and that it inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum — matching the keyed dose and mechanism. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A former 28-week-gestation infant, now 46 weeks postconceptional age and weighing 3.4 kg, is scheduled for outpatient unilateral inguinal hernia repair. The infant had a brief NICU course but is now feeding well, has no current apneic spells documented, and a recent hematocrit is 36%. The parents ask whether the baby can go home the same day after general anesthesia. Which is the most appropriate perioperative plan?

choices.text: Admit for at least 12 hours of continuous apnea and bradycardia monitoring postoperatively
choices.rationale: Because this ex-premature infant is well below the ~55–60 week postconceptional age threshold, the risk of postoperative apnea warrants overnight admission with at least 12 hours of apnea/bradycardia monitoring rather than same-day discharge.

choices.text: Proceed as a same-day outpatient with 2 hours of PACU monitoring before discharge
choices.rationale: Two hours is inadequate because postoperative apnea can occur up to 12 hours after surgery in former preterm infants, making early discharge unsafe.

choices.text: Discharge home if the infant is apnea-free in the PACU and parents have an apnea monitor
choices.rationale: Home apnea monitors do not substitute for inpatient observation, and PACU stability does not exclude delayed apnea in a high-risk former preterm infant.

choices.text: Avoid admission by performing the case under deep sevoflurane mask anesthesia alone
choices.rationale: Volatile-based general anesthesia does not eliminate—and does not reliably reduce—the postoperative apnea risk, so the need for monitored admission remains.

choices.text: Transfuse to a hematocrit above 40% so the infant can be safely discharged the same day
choices.rationale: While anemia increases apnea risk, transfusion is not indicated at a hematocrit of 36% and would not make same-day discharge of a 46-week-PCA former preterm infant safe.

explanation: Former preterm infants are at increased risk of postoperative apnea, which is greatest at lower postconceptional ages and is compounded by anemia and comorbidities. Apneic events can occur up to 12 hours after surgery, so infants below roughly 55–60 weeks PCA should be admitted for continuous apnea and bradycardia monitoring for at least 12 hours rather than treated as same-day outpatients. Spinal versus general anesthesia may lower early apnea severity, but it does not change the requirement for postoperative monitoring in this age group.
subtopic: Pediatric — postoperative apnea risk in the former preterm infant
reference_title: Apnea of Prematurity and Postoperative Apnea — OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Sound. A former 28-week infant now at 46 weeks PCA is well below the safe outpatient threshold (commonly 54-60 weeks PCA), so admission with continuous apnea/bradycardia monitoring for at least 12 hours is the single best answer per standard teaching (Cote meta-analysis, Miller/Cote texts) and the cited source. All distractors are genuinely inferior: 2h PACU discharge ignores delayed apnea up to 12h; a home apnea monitor does not substitute for inpatient monitoring; volatile GA does not eliminate apnea risk; and transfusion is not indicated at Hct 36% (anemia threshold for admission is typically Hct <30%) and would not make same-day discharge safe. The Hct 36% and 'no documented apneic spells' details are correctly framed as non-determinative traps; age drives the decision. The explanation's note that regional vs GA may reduce early apnea but does not remove the monitoring requirement aligns with current evidence (GAS trial). Rationales are all accurate.

REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia 'Apnea of Prematurity and Postoperative Apnea' page resolved and is directly on-topic. It corroborates the keyed answer: monitor at least 12 hours postoperatively, admit infants below ~55 weeks PCA, and anemia (Hct <30%) as an independent admission trigger. Citation is valid and matches the question content.

stem: A 71-year-old man is scheduled for elective open right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. His history includes hypertension, insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes, and a remote drug-eluting coronary stent placed 4 years ago for stable angina. He has had no chest pain since. He reports that he can carry two bags of groceries up two flights of stairs to his apartment without stopping, and he gardens and walks his dog daily. Vital signs and a resting ECG are unremarkable; creatinine is 1.1 mg/dL. The surgeon asks whether further cardiac testing is needed before surgery. Which factor most appropriately supports proceeding to surgery WITHOUT additional noninvasive cardiac stress testing?

choices.text: His Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) score places him in an elevated-risk category, which mandates stress testing regardless of symptoms
choices.rationale: Even when calculated risk is elevated, current ACC/AHA pathways do not mandate stress testing if functional capacity is adequate and testing would not change management, so an elevated index alone does not require further testing.

choices.text: His estimated functional capacity is at least 4 METs and he is asymptomatic, so additional testing is unlikely to change perioperative management
choices.rationale: Climbing two flights of stairs while carrying groceries reflects 4 or more METs; in an asymptomatic patient with adequate functional capacity, further stress testing rarely alters management and can proceed to surgery.

choices.text: His prior coronary stent obligates a preoperative stress test to confirm stent patency before any intermediate-risk surgery
choices.rationale: A remote, asymptomatic stent in a patient with good functional capacity does not by itself trigger stress testing, and routine 'patency' stress testing before noncardiac surgery is not recommended.

choices.text: Insulin-dependent diabetes is an absolute indication for preoperative pharmacologic stress testing in major abdominal surgery
choices.rationale: Insulin use is one RCRI predictor, but a single risk factor is not an absolute indication for stress testing, especially when functional capacity is adequate.

choices.text: Because open colectomy is a high-risk surgical procedure, stress testing is required for all patients over age 70
choices.rationale: Open intraperitoneal surgery is intermediate-risk, not high (vascular) risk, and neither the procedure category nor age alone mandates testing when functional capacity is adequate.

explanation: In the ACC/AHA stepwise approach to preoperative cardiac evaluation for noncardiac surgery, after excluding an acute coronary syndrome and estimating surgical/patient risk, functional capacity is the pivotal branch point. A patient who can achieve 4 or more METs (e.g., climbing two flights of stairs, brisk walking) without symptoms generally proceeds to surgery without further cardiac testing, because testing would be unlikely to change management. Additional noninvasive stress testing is reserved for patients with poor (<4 METs) or indeterminate functional capacity in whom the result would alter perioperative care. A single elevated risk factor, a remote asymptomatic stent, or the procedure category does not by itself override adequate functional capacity.
subtopic: Preoperative cardiac risk stratification — functional capacity (METs) and the decision to proceed versus test
reference_title: Cardiac Risk Assessment - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem (71M, remote asymptomatic DES from 4 yrs ago, HTN, insulin-dependent DM, normal Cr, elective open hemicolectomy=intermediate-risk, documented good functional capacity) tests the pivotal ACC/AHA branch point: an asymptomatic patient with adequate functional capacity (≥4 METs—carrying groceries up two flights clearly exceeds the ~4-MET one-flight benchmark) proceeds without stress testing because testing would not change management. Keyed answer B is the single best answer. All distractors are genuinely inferior and assert the opposite (testing is required/mandated): A—elevated RCRI does not mandate testing; C—routine stent 'patency' stress testing is not recommended and the stent is remote/stable/beyond any DAPT window; D—insulin use is one RCRI predictor, not an absolute indication; E—colectomy is intermediate-risk not high (vascular) risk, and age alone does not mandate testing (rationale even correctly relabels it intermediate-risk). Rationales and explanation are accurate and reflect the standard board-tested 2014 ACC/AHA stepwise framework. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537146/ resolves to 'Cardiac Risk Assessment' (StatPearls), which directly covers preoperative cardiac evaluation for noncardiac surgery, functional capacity/METs, the ACC/AHA stepwise algorithm, RCRI, and the stress-testing-versus-proceed decision—on-topic and supportive.

stem: A 68-year-old woman with long-standing hypertension and chronic kidney disease is undergoing elective lumbar laminectomy under general anesthesia. Her home medications include lisinopril, which she took on the morning of surgery per her primary physician's instruction. Shortly after induction with propofol and rocuronium and initiation of positive-pressure ventilation, her blood pressure falls to 64/38 mmHg with a heart rate of 70/min. She receives a 500 mL crystalloid bolus and repeated doses of IV phenylephrine and ephedrine with only transient, minimal improvement. There is no rash, no airway pressure change, and no ST changes. What is the most appropriate next pharmacologic intervention?

choices.text: Administer IV epinephrine infusion as the definitive treatment, since the picture is most consistent with anaphylaxis
choices.rationale: There is no rash, bronchospasm, or rising airway pressures, so anaphylaxis is unlikely, and escalating catecholamines is exactly the strategy that has already failed in this renin-angiotensin-blocked patient.

choices.text: Administer additional large-volume crystalloid and continue titrating phenylephrine, as the hypotension reflects inadequate preload
choices.rationale: The hypotension is poorly responsive to fluids and alpha-agonists because the angiotensin axis is blocked, so simply repeating the failed therapy is unlikely to restore pressure.

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia with additional propofol to treat presumed light anesthesia causing sympathetic instability
choices.rationale: Deepening anesthesia would worsen vasodilation and hypotension, and light anesthesia typically produces hypertension and tachycardia rather than refractory hypotension.

choices.text: Administer IV vasopressin, because ACE-inhibitor-blunted angiotensin responses make this hypotension resistant to catecholamines and responsive to the vasopressin axis
choices.rationale: With the renin-angiotensin system pharmacologically blocked, blood pressure becomes dependent on the vasopressin system, so vasopressin restores tone when catecholamines fail in ACE-inhibitor-associated refractory hypotension.

choices.text: Administer IV calcium chloride to reverse a presumed calcium-channel-blocker effect
choices.rationale: The patient is not on a calcium-channel blocker, and calcium is not the mechanism-directed therapy for angiotensin-axis-blockade hypotension.

explanation: Patients who continue an ACE inhibitor or ARB into the day of surgery are prone to profound hypotension after induction that is characteristically resistant to fluids and to conventional catecholamine vasopressors (phenylephrine, ephedrine, even norepinephrine). Blood pressure is normally maintained by three systems: the sympathetic nervous system, the renin-angiotensin system, and the vasopressin system. When the angiotensin axis is pharmacologically blocked and sympathetic responses are blunted by anesthesia, the vasopressin system becomes the effective rescue pathway, so vasopressin (or its analog) reliably restores vascular tone when catecholamines fail. This is also why many clinicians hold ACE inhibitors/ARBs on the morning of surgery in selected patients.
subtopic: Perioperative medication management — ACE inhibitor/ARB continuation and anesthesia-induced refractory hypotension

verdict.reason: CLINICAL (A): Sound and unambiguous. Keyed answer (IV vasopressin, index 3) is the single best choice for ACE-inhibitor/ARB-associated catecholamine-resistant refractory hypotension under GA. Mechanism is correct and current: anesthesia blunts the SNS, ACE-I blocks the renin-angiotensin axis, leaving the vasopressin system as the effective rescue pathway; alpha-receptor responsiveness is downregulated, explaining phenylephrine/ephedrine failure. Supported by systematic review PMID 28235174 (all studies showed significant BP improvement with vasopressin/analogues), the ASA Anesthesiology terlipressin-vs-norepinephrine trial, and multiple case reports. All distractors are genuinely inferior: anaphylaxis is excluded by absence of rash/bronchospasm/airway pressure rise/ST changes plus failed catecholamines; repeating fluids+phenylephrine is the already-failed therapy; deepening anesthesia worsens vasodilation; calcium chloride is wrong-mechanism since no CCB is on the medication list. Stem is internally consistent. Only nitpick (non-disqualifying): explanation's 'even norepinephrine' and 'BP becomes dependent on the vasopressin system' are slightly strong but consistent with cited literature. REFERENCE (B): The OpenAnesthesia URL resolves, loads full content, and is squarely on-topic — covers ACE-I perioperative management, fluid/vasopressor-resistant induction hypotension, and vasopressin preparedness. No reference problem. Both prongs sound -> keep.

stem: During the pre-use checkout of a modern anesthesia workstation, a student anesthesiologist assistant reviews the machine's built-in safety features before a case using an oxygen/nitrous oxide/sevoflurane technique. The attending asks the student to explain why, despite the proportioning (hypoxic guard) system, the inspired oxygen analyzer with a low-oxygen alarm is still considered an indispensable monitor. Which statement best explains the limitation of the proportioning system that the oxygen analyzer is designed to cover?

choices.text: The proportioning system links nitrous oxide and oxygen flows at the flowmeters but cannot detect a hypoxic mixture caused by an oxygen-nitrous pipeline crossover or a leak downstream of the flowmeters
choices.rationale: The proportioning (hypoxic guard) system only enforces a minimum O2:N2O ratio at the flow control valves, so it cannot catch a hypoxic mixture introduced by a crossed pipeline or a downstream leak, which is precisely what the inspired oxygen analyzer detects.

choices.text: The proportioning system measures the actual oxygen concentration delivered to the patient, so the oxygen analyzer is merely a redundant backup with no unique role
choices.rationale: The proportioning system controls flow ratios, not measured concentration, so it does not measure delivered oxygen and the analyzer is not merely redundant.

choices.text: The proportioning system fails whenever nitrous oxide is not in use, leaving the oxygen analyzer as the only safeguard during oxygen-air cases
choices.rationale: The proportioning system specifically governs the N2O:O2 relationship and is simply not engaged for oxygen-air mixtures; it does not 'fail,' and the claim misstates its function.

choices.text: The proportioning system shuts off all gas flow if oxygen supply pressure is lost, which makes a separate oxygen analyzer unnecessary
choices.rationale: Loss of oxygen supply pressure is handled by the fail-safe and oxygen-failure protection devices, not by the proportioning system, and none of these measure inspired oxygen concentration.

choices.text: The proportioning system continuously samples gas in the inspiratory limb and alarms on hypoxia, duplicating the oxygen analyzer's function
choices.rationale: The proportioning system acts at the flow control valves, not by sampling gas in the inspiratory limb, so it does not duplicate the analyzer's downstream measurement.

explanation: The proportioning (hypoxic guard) system mechanically or electronically links the nitrous oxide and oxygen flow control valves to prevent the operator from setting a fresh-gas mixture with less than roughly 25 percent oxygen at the flowmeters. However, it acts only at the level of the flow control valves: it cannot detect a hypoxic mixture produced by an oxygen-nitrous pipeline crossover, by administration of a third gas, or by a leak downstream of the flowmeters. The inspired (inspiratory limb) oxygen analyzer with a low-oxygen alarm is the only monitor that measures the actual oxygen concentration the patient receives, making it the essential last line of defense against delivery of a hypoxic gas mixture.
subtopic: Anesthesia machine safety — proportioning (hypoxic guard) system versus the inspired oxygen analyzer

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (index 0) is the single best choice and reflects standard teaching (Miller/Barash/Dorsch): the proportioning/hypoxic guard system (e.g., Link-25, S-ORC) acts only at the flow control valves and guarantees a minimum ~25% O2 at the flowmeter level. It cannot detect a hypoxic mixture from a pipeline/wall crossover, a third gas, or dilution/leak downstream of the flowmeters, so the inspired O2 analyzer is the true last line of defense measuring actual delivered FiO2. All four distractors contain definite factual errors and are genuinely inferior: index 1 falsely says the system measures delivered O2 concentration (it controls flow ratios); index 2 mischaracterizes it as 'failing' during O2-air cases (it is simply not engaged); index 3 conflates it with the fail-safe/oxygen failure protection device that responds to loss of O2 supply pressure; index 4 falsely attributes inspiratory-limb gas sampling to it. The explanation (mechanical/electronic linkage, ~25% O2 minimum, action at flow control valves, named limitations, analyzer as last line of defense) is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK572060/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Anesthesia Machine' article, which directly covers the anesthesia workstation, the mechanical/electronic linkage between the two flow control valves to prevent hypoxic mixtures, O2 flow sequencing downstream of other gases, and N2O:O2 proportioning. The citation is on-topic and appropriate.

stem: A 16-year-old girl is undergoing posterior spinal fusion for idiopathic scoliosis with continuous transcranial motor-evoked potential (tcMEP) and somatosensory-evoked potential (SSEP) monitoring. The neuromonitoring technologist reports that the MEP signals have become difficult to obtain. The anesthetic was recently transitioned to include a higher inspired concentration of sevoflurane plus a nitrous oxide and a continuous neuromuscular blockade infusion. Which anesthetic change is the MOST appropriate to preserve reliable motor-evoked potential monitoring?

choices.text: Increase the sevoflurane concentration further to ensure an immobile surgical field
choices.rationale: Halogenated volatile agents cause dose-dependent suppression of MEP amplitude, so increasing sevoflurane would further degrade, not improve, the signals.

choices.text: Continue the neuromuscular blockade infusion to eliminate movement artifact
choices.rationale: Neuromuscular blockade attenuates or abolishes the compound muscle action potential recorded as the MEP, so ongoing paralysis directly prevents reliable motor monitoring.

choices.text: Convert to a total intravenous anesthetic (e.g., propofol plus an opioid infusion) and discontinue neuromuscular blockade
choices.rationale: Propofol-opioid TIVA depresses evoked potentials far less than volatile agents, and stopping paralysis restores the muscle response, together giving the most reliable MEPs.

choices.text: Add a high-dose benzodiazepine infusion to reduce patient awareness
choices.rationale: Benzodiazepines depress evoked-potential amplitude and add little benefit, so they worsen rather than preserve monitoring quality.

choices.text: Increase the nitrous oxide concentration to allow a lower volatile requirement
choices.rationale: Nitrous oxide also depresses MEP and SSEP amplitude, particularly in combination with volatile agents, so raising it does not protect the signals.

explanation: Motor-evoked potentials are exquisitely sensitive to anesthetic depression. Halogenated volatile agents and nitrous oxide cause dose-dependent reductions in MEP amplitude, and even partial neuromuscular blockade attenuates the recorded compound muscle action potential. The preferred technique for cases requiring MEP monitoring is total intravenous anesthesia with propofol and an opioid (often supplemented with low-dose ketamine or dexmedetomidine), with neuromuscular blockade avoided or used only during induction. This maximizes the chance of detecting evolving spinal cord injury during corrective spine surgery.
subtopic: Anesthetic effects on intraoperative neuromonitoring (motor-evoked potentials)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 2 — convert to propofol/opioid TIVA and discontinue neuromuscular blockade) is the single best, unambiguous answer and reflects current standard of care for tcMEP monitoring in scoliosis surgery. All four distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: (A) increasing sevoflurane causes dose-dependent MEP suppression; (B) ongoing NMB attenuates/abolishes the recorded CMAP; (D) benzodiazepines depress EP amplitude with no benefit; (E) nitrous oxide also depresses MEP/SSEP, especially combined with volatiles. The explanation correctly notes TIVA with propofol + opioid (± low-dose ketamine or dexmedetomidine) with NMB avoided. The 'low-dose' hedge on dexmedetomidine is appropriate (higher doses can depress MEPs). No medical inaccuracy. REFERENCE: reference_url (https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/evoked-potentials/) resolves to an active OpenAnesthesia article on intraoperative neurophysiologic monitoring (updated Jan 2023). It is directly on-topic and explicitly supports every keyed point: TIVA with propofol/opioids ± ketamine preferred for MEP, volatile <0.5 MAC, nitrous oxide should be avoided, and NMBAs avoided until baseline MEPs obtained. Both clinical and reference dimensions are sound.

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 35 weeks gestation is admitted with severe preeclampsia (BP 168/112 mmHg, 3+ proteinuria) and started on an intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion for seizure prophylaxis. Three hours later, while you are evaluating her for possible neuraxial labor analgesia, she becomes drowsy and her speech is slurred. On exam, her patellar and biceps reflexes are absent, respiratory rate is 9/min with shallow breaths, and SpO2 is 92% on room air. Her urine output over the last 2 hours has been 15 mL. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate pharmacologic intervention?

choices.text: Administer intravenous calcium gluconate 1 g
choices.rationale: Correct: loss of deep tendon reflexes with respiratory depression signals magnesium toxicity, and IV calcium directly antagonizes magnesium at the neuromuscular junction and myocardium as the immediate antidote.

choices.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg
choices.rationale: Incorrect: flumazenil reverses benzodiazepine sedation, but this patient's depressed sensorium and areflexia are due to magnesium, not a benzodiazepine.

choices.text: Administer intravenous naloxone 0.4 mg
choices.rationale: Incorrect: naloxone reverses opioid-induced respiratory depression, but no opioid has been given and the areflexia points specifically to hypermagnesemia.

choices.text: Administer a 500 mL crystalloid bolus and continue the magnesium infusion
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a fluid bolus does not antagonize magnesium and continuing the infusion would worsen toxicity in a patient with reduced renal clearance.

choices.text: Administer intravenous furosemide 40 mg as the sole intervention
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although diuresis can enhance magnesium elimination, it does not provide the immediate cardioprotection needed and is not the first-line emergency treatment for symptomatic toxicity.

explanation: Loss of deep tendon reflexes is an early clinical marker of magnesium toxicity (typically around serum levels of 10 mEq/L), and progression to respiratory depression and paralysis follows at higher levels (~15 mEq/L). Oliguria reduces renal magnesium clearance and predisposes preeclamptic patients on magnesium infusions to accumulation and toxicity. The immediate antidote is intravenous calcium (gluconate or chloride), which antagonizes magnesium at the neuromuscular junction and heart; the infusion must be stopped and ventilation supported as needed.
subtopic: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy: magnesium sulfate toxicity

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Stem is a textbook presentation of magnesium sulfate toxicity in severe preeclampsia—loss of patellar/biceps DTRs (~10 mEq/L), respiratory depression (RR 9), drowsiness/slurred speech, and oliguria (15 mL/2h) reducing renal Mg clearance. Keyed answer, IV calcium gluconate, is the standard immediate antidote that antagonizes magnesium at the neuromuscular junction and myocardium—correct and current. All distractors are genuinely inferior: flumazenil (no benzodiazepine given), naloxone (no opioid given, and areflexia is not opioid-mediated), crystalloid bolus + continuing the Mg infusion (continuing infusion worsens toxicity), and furosemide alone (enhances elimination but provides no immediate cardioprotection). The stem asks specifically for the immediate PHARMACOLOGIC intervention, and the rationale/explanation correctly note the infusion must also be stopped and ventilation supported—so the framing is not misleading. Explanation's serum-level thresholds (DTR loss ~10 mEq/L, respiratory depression/paralysis ~15 mEq/L) are accurate. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554593/ resolves to StatPearls 'Magnesium Toxicity' and directly supports the stem, stating DTRs/muscle weakness lost at 10 mEq/L, respiratory paralysis at 15 mEq/L, and 'intravenous calcium gluconate can displace and neutralize the effects of magnesium.' Citation is accurate and on-topic. Keep.

stem: A 29-year-old G3P2 woman undergoes induction of labor at 39 weeks for an uncomplicated pregnancy. Shortly after artificial rupture of membranes during an otherwise normal labor, she suddenly becomes acutely dyspneic and agitated, then loses consciousness. Her SpO2 falls to 70%, BP is 60/30 mmHg, and the fetal heart tracing shows a prolonged deceleration. Within minutes she develops generalized oozing from her IV sites and the vaginal canal; laboratory studies return a fibrinogen of 80 mg/dL and a markedly prolonged PT and aPTT. Which of the following best describes the most appropriate priority of management for the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Immediate empiric systemic thrombolysis with alteplase
choices.rationale: Incorrect: thrombolysis would be catastrophic in a patient already hemorrhaging with consumptive coagulopathy and is not indicated for amniotic fluid embolism.

choices.text: High-quality cardiopulmonary support with aggressive correction of coagulopathy using blood products, and prompt delivery if maternal arrest occurs
choices.rationale: Correct: amniotic fluid embolism is managed with immediate high-quality resuscitation, oxygenation and hemodynamic support, aggressive transfusion to correct DIC, and perimortem cesarean delivery if maternal cardiac arrest occurs.

choices.text: Withhold blood products and give tranexamic acid alone until a definitive diagnosis is confirmed by a serum assay
choices.rationale: Incorrect: amniotic fluid embolism is a clinical diagnosis with no confirmatory serum test, and withholding blood products in life-threatening DIC would be dangerous.

choices.text: Administer protamine sulfate to reverse presumed heparin effect
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the coagulopathy is consumptive (DIC) rather than heparin-induced, so protamine has no role here.

choices.text: Place the patient in steep reverse Trendelenburg and restrict fluids to limit pulmonary edema
choices.rationale: Incorrect: this worsens hypotension in a patient with cardiovascular collapse, who instead requires hemodynamic support and resuscitation.

explanation: Amniotic fluid embolism classically presents with the abrupt triad of cardiorespiratory collapse (hypoxia/hypotension) and disseminated intravascular coagulation, often beginning during labor, delivery, or the immediate postpartum period. It is a clinical diagnosis with no definitive confirmatory test, so management is supportive and time-critical: secure the airway and oxygenate, support the circulation, and aggressively transfuse blood products to treat the consumptive coagulopathy. If maternal cardiac arrest occurs, perimortem cesarean delivery within minutes improves both maternal resuscitation and neonatal outcome.
subtopic: Obstetric emergencies: amniotic fluid embolism

verdict.reason: Clinical: Stem is a textbook amniotic fluid embolism presentation (abrupt cardiorespiratory collapse during labor right after ARM, hypoxia, profound hypotension, fetal bradycardia, plus DIC with fibrinogen 80 and prolonged PT/aPTT). Keyed answer B (high-quality cardiopulmonary support, aggressive blood-product correction of DIC, perimortem cesarean if maternal arrest) is the single best answer and matches SMFM/current AFE management. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A (alteplase) is catastrophic in active DIC/hemorrhage and AFE is not thrombotic PE; C is wrong for withholding blood products and awaiting a nonexistent confirmatory serum test in life-threatening DIC; D (protamine) is irrelevant with no heparin and consumptive coagulopathy; E (reverse Trendelenburg/fluid restriction) worsens hypotension in cardiovascular collapse. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. Reference: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia amniotic-fluid-embolism page resolves and is squarely on-topic, confirming clinical diagnosis with no definitive test, supportive resuscitation, transfusion of FFP/cryo/blood products for the consumptive coagulopathy, and the role of delivery. No clinical or reference problems found.

stem: A 33-year-old laboring parturient requests epidural analgesia. After negative aspiration and a negative test dose, the anesthetist begins incremental injection of 0.25% bupivacaine through the epidural catheter. Partway through the loading dose the patient reports perioral numbness and tinnitus, then has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Her heart rate falls to 38/min with widened QRS complexes, and she becomes profoundly hypotensive. The seizure is treated and ventilation is supported. In addition to standard ACLS modifications, which of the following is the most appropriate next pharmacologic step?

choices.text: Administer a 20% lipid emulsion bolus of approximately 1.5 mL/kg of lean body weight, followed by an infusion
choices.rationale: Correct: this presentation is local anesthetic systemic toxicity with cardiotoxicity, and intravenous lipid emulsion is the specific treatment, given as a bolus (~1.5 mL/kg lean body weight) followed by an infusion.

choices.text: Administer high-dose epinephrine boluses of 1 mg as in standard adult ACLS
choices.rationale: Incorrect: in local anesthetic systemic toxicity, epinephrine should be given in reduced doses (e.g., ≤1 mcg/kg) because high doses can impair resuscitation and worsen arrhythmias.

choices.text: Administer intravenous vasopressin as the first-line vasopressor
choices.rationale: Incorrect: vasopressin is specifically discouraged in local anesthetic systemic toxicity resuscitation and is not the antidote for the underlying toxicity.

choices.text: Administer a calcium channel blocker to narrow the QRS complex
choices.rationale: Incorrect: calcium channel blockers and beta-blockers are avoided in local anesthetic systemic toxicity because they can further depress an already compromised myocardium.

choices.text: Administer flumazenil to reverse the seizure activity
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the seizure is caused by local anesthetic central nervous system toxicity, not a benzodiazepine, so flumazenil is ineffective and inappropriate.

explanation: Premonitory CNS symptoms (perioral numbness, tinnitus) followed by seizure and cardiovascular collapse with conduction abnormalities are the hallmark progression of local anesthetic systemic toxicity, and bupivacaine is notably cardiotoxic. Management centers on airway control and oxygenation to prevent acidosis plus early intravenous lipid emulsion: a roughly 1.5 mL/kg bolus of 20% emulsion (lean body weight) followed by an infusion, with the total dose capped to avoid lipid overload. ACLS is modified by using reduced-dose epinephrine and avoiding vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and local anesthetics such as lidocaine.
subtopic: Neuraxial analgesia complications: local anesthetic systemic toxicity

verdict.reason: Clinical: The stem is a textbook LAST progression — premonitory CNS symptoms (perioral numbness, tinnitus) followed by seizure then cardiovascular collapse with widened QRS and bradycardia, with bupivacaine as the prototypical cardiotoxic agent. The keyed answer (choice A, 20% lipid emulsion ~1.5 mL/kg bolus then infusion) is the single best, specific antidotal therapy per ASRA 2020. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate and current: high-dose 1 mg epinephrine is wrong (ASRA recommends reduced dosing, ≤1 mcg/kg); vasopressin is explicitly discouraged in LAST; calcium channel blockers/beta-blockers are avoided; flumazenil is inappropriate since the seizure is LA-induced, not benzodiazepine-induced. The explanation is correct, including airway/oxygenation to prevent acidosis and the lipid dose cap. The 'lean body weight' phrasing for the bolus is an accepted convention (Weinberg/LipidRescue) and not an error. Reference: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia LAST page resolved successfully (not a 404), is exactly on-topic, and corroborates every key teaching point — 1.5 mL/kg lipid bolus, dose cap ~12 mL/kg, reduced epinephrine ≤1 mcg/kg, and avoidance of vasopressin and local anesthetics for arrhythmias. Both clinical content and citation are sound.

stem: A 34-year-old woman (ASA II, BMI 24) undergoes outpatient knee arthroscopy under general anesthesia at a freestanding ambulatory surgery center. Two hours after arrival in the post-anesthesia care unit she is alert and conversant. Her vital signs are stable and within 15% of baseline. She has had one episode of nausea that resolved with a single dose of ondansetron and has not vomited. Her surgical dressing is dry with no expanding hematoma, and her pain is 2/10 on oral analgesics. She is able to sit and walk to the bathroom with steady gait and no dizziness. Using the Post-Anesthetic Discharge Scoring System (PADSS), which single finding, if present instead, would most appropriately prevent home discharge at this time?

choices.text: Vital signs within 20% of preoperative baseline
choices.rationale: PADSS awards full points for vital signs within 20% of baseline, so this finding would not preclude discharge.

choices.text: Minimal nausea controlled with oral medication
choices.rationale: Mild, controlled nausea/vomiting still earns a passing PADSS vital-symptom score and does not by itself block discharge.

choices.text: Ambulation with a steady gait and no orthostatic dizziness
choices.rationale: Steady, dizziness-free ambulation is exactly the response PADSS rewards and is a marker of readiness, not a contraindication.

choices.text: Surgical-site bleeding requiring a dressing change and continued oozing
choices.rationale: Ongoing surgical bleeding that requires intervention scores low on the PADSS bleeding domain and is a legitimate reason to delay discharge.

choices.text: Pain rated 2/10 and controlled with oral analgesics
choices.rationale: Low pain controlled by oral agents earns full PADSS points and supports, rather than prevents, discharge.

explanation: The modified PADSS scores five domains—vital signs, ambulation, nausea/vomiting, pain, and surgical bleeding—each 0 to 2, with a total of 9 or greater required for home discharge. Vital signs within 20% of baseline, mild controlled nausea, steady ambulation, and well-controlled pain all earn passing scores. Continued surgical-site bleeding that requires a dressing change scores low and should delay discharge until hemostasis is confirmed.
subtopic: Discharge readiness and recovery scoring (PADSS) after ambulatory surgery

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The modified PADSS (Chung) scores five domains 0-2 each (vital signs, ambulation/activity, nausea-vomiting, pain, surgical bleeding) with total >=9 required for home discharge — accurately reflected in the explanation. Choice D (surgical-site bleeding requiring a dressing change with continued oozing) is the only finding among the options that lowers a domain score (moderate/severe bleeding = 1 or 0 points), correctly preventing discharge. All four distractors (VS within 20% of baseline, minimal controlled nausea, steady dizziness-free ambulation, pain 2/10 on oral agents) each earn full/passing PADSS points and support discharge, so they are genuinely inferior. The stem's 'within 15% of baseline' is consistent with choice A's 'within 20%' top tier — no inconsistency. Choice B's rationale phrase 'vital-symptom score' is slightly loose wording but the conclusion is correct; not a content error. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/pacu-discharge-criteria/ resolves and is directly on-topic — it describes PADSS adding ambulation, nausea/vomiting, pain, and bleeding to recovery assessment, lists VS within 20% of baseline and 'no significant bleeding' as discharge criteria, matching the question's content. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 28-year-old woman is scheduled for outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She is a non-smoker, has a history of motion sickness, and reports severe nausea after a prior anesthetic. She takes no chronic opioids. The anesthesiologist plans a general anesthetic and wants to minimize her risk of postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV), which would threaten same-day discharge. Based on the Apfel simplified risk score and current prophylaxis principles, which strategy is the MOST appropriate?

choices.text: Administer a single antiemetic and otherwise proceed with a standard volatile-based, opioid-heavy anesthetic
choices.rationale: A patient with three to four Apfel risk factors needs multimodal prophylaxis plus risk reduction, not a single agent layered on a high-emetogenic technique.

choices.text: Use a propofol-based total intravenous anesthetic, minimize opioids and volatile agents, and give two or more antiemetics from different classes
choices.rationale: Combining a baseline-risk-lowering technique (TIVA, opioid sparing, avoiding volatiles) with multimodal prophylaxis is the guideline-endorsed approach for high-risk patients.

choices.text: Withhold all prophylaxis and treat nausea only if it occurs in the PACU
choices.rationale: A wait-and-treat approach is inappropriate for a high-risk patient and predisposes to unplanned admission for intractable vomiting.

choices.text: Give dexamethasone plus a second dose of the same 5-HT3 antagonist she received before
choices.rationale: Repeating a drug from the same class adds little benefit; effective multimodal prophylaxis combines agents from different mechanistic classes.

choices.text: Premedicate with a high-dose opioid to ensure deep analgesia and reduce movement-related nausea
choices.rationale: Perioperative opioids increase, rather than decrease, PONV risk, so an opioid-heavy plan is counterproductive.

explanation: The Apfel score counts female sex, non-smoking status, history of PONV or motion sickness, and anticipated postoperative opioids; this patient has at least three risk factors, placing her at high risk. Guidelines recommend lowering baseline risk (propofol TIVA, opioid sparing, avoiding volatile agents and nitrous oxide) and adding two or more prophylactic antiemetics from different classes such as a 5-HT3 antagonist, dexamethasone, and a neurokinin-1 antagonist or droperidol. Effective PONV control is especially important in ambulatory patients because intractable vomiting is a leading cause of unanticipated admission.
subtopic: PONV risk stratification and prophylaxis in the outpatient setting

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Apfel simplified score = 4 factors here (female, non-smoker, history of PONV/motion sickness, anticipated postoperative opioids for lap chole), placing her at high risk. The keyed answer (propofol TIVA + opioid/volatile sparing to lower baseline risk PLUS >=2 antiemetics from different classes) is exactly the Fourth Consensus Guidelines (Gan 2020) approach for high-risk patients and is the single best answer. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: single agent on an opioid-heavy volatile technique (inadequate), withholding prophylaxis (inappropriate for high risk), repeating a same-class 5-HT3 plus dexamethasone (same mechanistic class adds little), and high-dose opioid premedication (opioids increase PONV). The 'takes no chronic opioids' detail does not remove the anticipated postoperative-opioid factor, and management is identical whether scored at 3 or 4 factors, so the answer is robust. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK500029/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Postoperative Nausea' chapter, which directly covers PONV risk factors (non-smoking females with PONV/motion sickness history), propofol TIVA/avoidance of inhalational agents, opioid-sparing multimodal therapy, and multimodal antiemetics (5-HT3 antagonists, droperidol, dexamethasone, NK-1/aprepitant). Citation is valid and on-topic.

subtopic: TURP syndrome — dilutional hyponatremia from irrigant absorption (Urology)
stem: A 74-year-old man with a 90-g prostate is undergoing monopolar transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) under spinal anesthesia with continuous glycine 1.5% bladder irrigation. About 55 minutes into a resection involving several large open venous sinuses, he becomes restless and complains of nausea and visual blurring, then has a brief generalized seizure. Blood pressure is 168/96 mmHg, heart rate 52/min, and SpO2 96%. A stat metabolic panel returns a serum sodium of 112 mmol/L. Which intervention is the most appropriate immediate next step?

choices.text: Continue resection but switch the irrigant to 0.9% normal saline to limit further sodium loss
choices.rationale: Monopolar resection cannot use an electrolyte-containing irrigant because saline disperses the cutting current; more fundamentally, the surgery must stop and the symptomatic hyponatremia be treated, not continued.

choices.text: Give a 2-liter bolus of lactated Ringer's solution to restore intravascular volume
choices.rationale: TURP syndrome is a dilutional, fluid-overloaded state, so a large isotonic crystalloid bolus would worsen the volume overload and only marginally raise sodium.

choices.text: Stop the resection, secure the airway as needed, and administer hypertonic (3%) saline with a loop diuretic while monitoring sodium
choices.rationale: Acute symptomatic hyponatremia with seizures is a neurologic emergency requiring controlled correction with 3% saline, halting ongoing irrigant absorption by stopping surgery, and managing volume overload with a diuretic.

choices.text: Administer intravenous mannitol to draw the absorbed glycine out of the cells
choices.rationale: Mannitol promotes free-water diuresis but does not reliably correct severe symptomatic hyponatremia and adds an osmotic load; hypertonic saline is the definitive therapy here.

choices.text: Restrict all intravenous fluids and observe, since the sodium will autocorrect once irrigation stops
choices.rationale: Watchful waiting is unacceptable in a patient already seizing from a sodium of 112 mmol/L, who needs active correction rather than passive observation.

explanation: TURP syndrome results from systemic absorption of large volumes of hypotonic, electrolyte-free irrigant (glycine, sorbitol, or mannitol) through open prostatic venous sinuses, producing acute dilutional hyponatremia and fluid overload. The classic triad of neurologic changes (confusion, seizures), cardiovascular signs (hypertension with reflex bradycardia), and visual disturbance (worsened by glycine, an inhibitory retinal neurotransmitter) should prompt immediate action. Management of symptomatic hyponatremia is to stop the procedure, support the airway, and correct sodium with 3% saline plus a loop diuretic for the volume overload, correcting at a controlled rate to avoid osmotic demyelination.
reference_title: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Stem is a textbook severe symptomatic TURP syndrome (monopolar TURP, glycine 1.5%, open sinuses ~55 min, seizure, hypertension with reflex bradycardia, Na 112). Keyed answer C (stop resection, secure airway, 3% hypertonic saline + loop diuretic, monitor Na) is the single best answer and the standard of care for seizing acute hyponatremia. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: A is doubly wrong (monopolar cannot use saline irrigant as it disperses cutting current, and surgery must stop); B (2L LR bolus) worsens the volume-overloaded state and only marginally raises Na; D (mannitol) is not definitive for severe symptomatic hyponatremia and adds osmotic load; E (observe) is unacceptable during active seizure. Finer points are accurate and current: glycine is an inhibitory retinal neurotransmitter causing transient visual disturbance; pairing hypertonic saline WITH a loop diuretic is specifically appropriate in TURP syndrome because it is a fluid-overloaded (not euvolemic) state; the ODS/controlled-correction caution is correct. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560884/ resolves to StatPearls 'Transurethral Resection of the Prostate,' which directly covers TUR syndrome, glycine/sorbitol/mannitol non-conducting irrigants, dilutional hyponatremia, symptom thresholds (≤115 mEq/L), and management with furosemide plus cautious 3% hypertonic saline — fully on-topic and corroborating the keyed answer. No reference, clinical, or ambiguity issue.

subtopic: Bone cement implantation syndrome during cemented hip arthroplasty (Orthopedic)
stem: An 84-year-old woman with an ASA physical status of 3 is undergoing cemented hemiarthroplasty for a displaced femoral neck fracture under general anesthesia. Just as the surgeon pressurizes the methylmethacrylate cement and impacts the femoral prosthesis into the canal, her blood pressure falls from 124/70 to 72/40 mmHg, end-tidal CO2 drops from 34 to 21 mmHg, and SpO2 declines to 88% despite an FiO2 of 0.5. Which mechanism best explains this constellation of findings?

choices.text: An acute IgE-mediated anaphylactic reaction to the methylmethacrylate monomer
choices.rationale: True IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to cement is rare and would typically include bronchospasm and urticaria; the tight temporal link to canal pressurization points instead to embolic and vasoactive mechanisms.

choices.text: Embolization of marrow, fat, and cement debris with humoral mediator release, causing increased pulmonary vascular resistance and right heart strain
choices.rationale: Pressurization forces marrow, fat, and cement emboli into the venous circulation, raising pulmonary vascular resistance and impairing right ventricular output, which produces the simultaneous hypotension, hypoxemia, and abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2.

choices.text: Direct negative inotropy from systemic absorption of residual volatile anesthetic concentrated in the cement
choices.rationale: Volatile agents are not stored or concentrated in bone cement, and this would not explain the sharp drop in end-tidal CO2 from dead-space ventilation.

choices.text: Reflex bradycardic hypotension from acute blood loss during reaming of the femoral canal
choices.rationale: Hemorrhage can cause hypotension, but it would not abruptly lower both end-tidal CO2 and SpO2 in the way pulmonary embolic obstruction does, and the timing coincides with cementation rather than reaming.

choices.text: Transfusion-related acute lung injury from recently administered packed red cells
choices.rationale: TRALI presents as hypoxemic non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema typically within hours of transfusion, not as instantaneous cardiovascular collapse at the moment of prosthesis insertion.

explanation: Bone cement implantation syndrome (BCIS) is characterized by hypoxia, hypotension, arrhythmias, and sometimes cardiac arrest occurring around cementation, prosthesis insertion, or joint reduction. Pressurizing the femoral canal embolizes marrow, fat, and cement debris into the pulmonary circulation and triggers humoral mediator release, acutely increasing pulmonary vascular resistance and causing right ventricular strain; the sudden fall in end-tidal CO2 reflects increased alveolar dead space from this pulmonary embolic obstruction. High-risk patients (advanced age, ASA 3 to 4, significant cardiopulmonary disease, fracture surgery) warrant a high FiO2, ensured euvolemia, and readiness to support the right heart with vasopressors at the moment of cementation.
reference_title: Methyl Methacrylate Complications and Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome (OpenAnesthesia)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound. Classic BCIS presentation (cemented hemiarthroplasty, elderly ASA 3 fracture patient, simultaneous hypotension + hypoxia + abrupt EtCO2 fall at the moment of pressurization/impaction). Keyed answer B (embolization of marrow/fat/cement debris with humoral mediator release -> increased PVR -> RV strain) is the single best, current mechanism, and the EtCO2 drop is correctly attributed to increased alveolar dead space from pulmonary vascular obstruction. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A is IgE anaphylaxis specifically (rare, would feature bronchospasm/urticaria, wrong mechanism); C (volatile concentrated in cement) is physiologically false; D (reflex bradycardia from reaming blood loss) has wrong timing and would not drop EtCO2/SpO2 this way; E (TRALI) is the wrong timescale (hours, not instantaneous). Management pearl (high FiO2, euvolemia, vasopressor readiness) is accurate. One nuance: the cited OpenAnesthesia page leans on the MMA-monomer/mediator theory while B is embolic-predominant, but B's blended embolic+mediator model is the modern consensus and remains the unambiguous best answer since A frames the monomer pathway as IgE anaphylaxis. REFERENCE: WebFetched successfully (HTTP 200); page is genuinely titled 'Methyl Methacrylate Complications and Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome' (OpenAnesthesia, updated May 2023) and directly covers BCIS, hypoxia/hypotension, pulmonary embolism from PMMA, and decreased EtCO2 with embolization. On-topic and supportive. Both checks pass.

subtopic: Lithotomy positioning injury — well-leg compartment syndrome and peroneal neuropraxia (GYN/Urology)
stem: A healthy 41-year-old woman undergoes a 5-hour robotic-assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy in steep Trendelenburg with both legs in candy-cane stirrups in the lithotomy position. In the recovery unit she reports severe, deep, unrelenting pain in the right calf that is markedly worse than expected and is not relieved by opioids; the calf is tense and tender, and passive dorsiflexion of the foot reproduces excruciating pain. Distal pulses are palpable. Which complication is most likely, and what is the primary driver of this injury?

choices.text: Common peroneal nerve neuropraxia from compression at the fibular head, driven by pressure from the stirrup post
choices.rationale: Peroneal neuropraxia is the most frequent lithotomy nerve injury but presents with foot drop and lateral leg numbness rather than tense, exquisitely painful calf compartments with pain on passive stretch.

choices.text: Deep venous thrombosis of the right calf, driven by venous stasis during the prolonged procedure
choices.rationale: DVT can cause calf pain and swelling, but the hallmark severe pain on passive muscle stretch out of proportion to findings indicates a compartment process, and DVT does not cause a tense, tender compartment with this stretch sign.

choices.text: Femoral nerve injury from excessive hip flexion and external rotation in the stirrups
choices.rationale: Femoral neuropathy produces quadriceps weakness and anterior thigh sensory loss, not an acutely tense, painful calf with pain on passive ankle dorsiflexion.

choices.text: Well-leg compartment syndrome, driven by reduced calf perfusion from elevation above the heart plus external stirrup compression during the prolonged case
choices.rationale: Elevating the legs above the heart lowers calf arterial perfusion pressure while the stirrups externally compress the compartments over a prolonged case, producing the classic out-of-proportion pain and pain on passive stretch with preserved distal pulses.

choices.text: Saphenous nerve compression at the medial tibial condyle from the leg holder
choices.rationale: Saphenous nerve compression causes medial leg and ankle paresthesias, not a tense, exquisitely painful calf compartment with pain on passive stretch, which defines compartment syndrome.

explanation: Well-leg compartment syndrome is a rare but limb-threatening complication of the prolonged lithotomy position: elevating the legs above the level of the heart reduces arterial perfusion pressure to the calf while external compression from stirrups raises intracompartmental pressure, and steep Trendelenburg further impairs venous return. The cardinal sign is pain out of proportion to expected findings, worsened by passive stretch of the involved muscles, with distal pulses often preserved until late. Risk rises sharply with operative times beyond several hours; prevention includes intermittently lowering and repositioning the legs, adequate padding, and minimizing total time in lithotomy, and established compartment syndrome requires emergent fasciotomy.
reference_title: Compartment Syndrome Due to Patient Positioning (StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 3, well-leg compartment syndrome) is unambiguously the single best answer. The vignette is pathognomonic for WLCS: severe deep pain out of proportion unrelieved by opioids, pain on passive stretch (passive ankle dorsiflexion stretching the deep posterior/superficial calf compartments), tense/tender calf, and preserved distal pulses (lost only late) — all in the setting of 5-hour lithotomy with candy-cane stirrups and steep Trendelenburg, the highest-risk scenario. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: peroneal neuropraxia (painless foot drop + lateral leg numbness), DVT (no pain-on-passive-stretch with tense compartment; out-of-proportion pain points to compartment process), femoral neuropathy (quadriceps weakness/anterior thigh sensory loss), and saphenous compression (pure medial sensory) each describe a different, incorrect entity — only one option names a compartment syndrome. The keyed pathophysiology (reduced calf arterial perfusion from elevation above heart + external stirrup compression over a prolonged case, with Trendelenburg impairing venous return) matches current literature (PMC9653962 review). One minor nuance — reperfusion injury upon lowering the leg is a recognized contributor not mentioned — does not affect answer selection or correctness, since the keyed driver is accurate and is the single best framing. Explanation (emergent fasciotomy, risk rising beyond several hours, prevention by intermittent lowering/padding) is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553906/ resolves to StatPearls 'Compartment Syndrome Due to Patient Positioning,' which explicitly covers positioning-related compartment syndrome, identifies lithotomy as highest-risk, cites >5h procedures and fasciotomy treatment — directly on-topic and supporting the question. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 woman receives a labor epidural. During placement the resident notes a sudden loss of resistance with free flow of clear fluid through the Tuohy needle, consistent with inadvertent dural puncture with the 17-gauge needle. She delivers vaginally the next day. On the second postpartum day she develops a severe bilateral fronto-occipital headache that is markedly worse when she sits upright and nearly resolves when she lies flat, accompanied by neck stiffness and mild photophobia. She is afebrile with a normal neurologic exam. Which feature most strongly supports the diagnosis of post-dural puncture headache rather than a more dangerous cause?

choices.text: The headache is clearly positional, worsening within minutes of sitting or standing and improving when supine
choices.rationale: A postural headache that worsens upright and improves recumbent is the hallmark of CSF hypotension from a dural puncture and is the defining feature of PDPH.

choices.text: The presence of mild photophobia and neck stiffness
choices.rationale: While these can accompany PDPH, photophobia and neck stiffness are nonspecific and also occur in meningitis and subarachnoid hemorrhage, so they do not distinguish PDPH.

choices.text: The bilateral fronto-occipital location of the pain
choices.rationale: Headache location is nonspecific; many primary and secondary headaches are bilateral and fronto-occipital, so location alone does not confirm PDPH.

choices.text: Onset on the second postpartum day rather than immediately after the puncture
choices.rationale: Timing of onset within a few days is typical but not diagnostic, as cortical vein thrombosis and other postpartum headaches share a similar delayed onset.

choices.text: The use of a large-bore 17-gauge needle for the puncture
choices.rationale: A large needle raises the probability and severity of PDPH but is a risk factor, not a clinical feature that confirms the diagnosis at the bedside.

explanation: Post-dural puncture headache results from continued CSF leak through the dural rent, lowering CSF pressure and causing traction on pain-sensitive structures. The pathognomonic feature is its postural nature: the headache worsens within minutes of assuming an upright position and is relieved by lying flat. Because postpartum patients are also at risk for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, preeclampsia/eclampsia, and meningitis, a non-positional headache, fever, or focal neurologic deficit should prompt imaging rather than presumptive PDPH treatment. First-line management is conservative (analgesia, hydration), with an epidural blood patch reserved for severe or persistent symptoms.
subtopic: Neuraxial complications — post-dural puncture headache

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 0, postural nature) is the single best answer. The orthostatic quality—worsening upright, resolving supine—is the defining/discriminating feature of PDPH per ICHD-3 and StatPearls, and is precisely what separates it from the dangerous postpartum mimics (CVST, SAH, meningitis, preeclampsia). All distractors are genuinely inferior: photophobia/neck stiffness and bilateral fronto-occipital location are nonspecific and overlap with meningitis/SAH; day-2 onset is typical (12-72h) but shared with CVST and other postpartum headaches; the 17-gauge needle is a risk factor, not a bedside confirmatory feature. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current (continued CSF leak/traction mechanism, conservative first-line management with epidural blood patch for severe/persistent cases). Stem is internally consistent and unambiguous. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430925/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Postdural Puncture Headache' chapter, directly on-topic—covers the postural headache, clinical diagnosis, differential (meningitis, preeclampsia, etc.), larger-caliber-needle risk factor, and epidural blood patch treatment. No clinical or reference problems found.

stem: A 64-year-old man with septic shock is in the OR for emergent bowel resection. To guide vasopressor and fluid therapy, the anesthetist floats a pulmonary artery (Swan-Ganz) catheter through a right internal jugular introducer. As the catheter is advanced and the balloon is inflated, the transduced pressure tracing abruptly changes from a waveform with a mean of about 4 mmHg and small a and v waves to a pulsatile tracing reading roughly 28/3 mmHg with a sharp upstroke and a diastolic pressure near zero. At what anatomic location is the catheter tip when this new waveform appears?

choices.text: Right atrium
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the right atrial tracing is the low-amplitude waveform (mean ~2-6 mmHg with a, c, and v waves) seen before this change, not the pulsatile 28/3 mmHg trace.

choices.text: Right ventricle
choices.rationale: Correct — a sharply pulsatile waveform with a high systolic pressure (~25-30 mmHg), a near-zero diastolic pressure, and a steep systolic upstroke is the classic right ventricular tracing.

choices.text: Pulmonary artery
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the pulmonary artery tracing has a similar systolic pressure but a HIGHER diastolic pressure (~8-12 mmHg) with a dicrotic notch, so its pulse pressure is narrower than the RV tracing shown.

choices.text: Pulmonary capillary wedge (occlusion) position
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the wedge tracing is a low, damped waveform with a and v waves resembling left atrial pressure, not a high-amplitude pulsatile trace.

choices.text: Superior vena cava
choices.rationale: Incorrect — the SVC produces a low venous waveform essentially identical to the right atrial tracing, not a ventricular-pressure tracing with a wide pulse pressure.

explanation: As a pulmonary artery catheter is advanced, each chamber produces a characteristic waveform: a low-amplitude venous trace in the RA/SVC, then an abruptly pulsatile RV trace with a high systolic and near-zero diastolic pressure, then a PA trace with the same systolic pressure but an elevated diastolic pressure and a dicrotic notch, and finally a damped wedge trace. Recognizing the RV waveform matters clinically because the catheter tip sitting in the RV can provoke ventricular ectopy and arrhythmias, so the operator should promptly advance into the PA. The step-up in diastolic pressure (near zero to ~8-12 mmHg) is the key feature distinguishing the PA tracing from the RV tracing.
subtopic: Invasive hemodynamic monitoring — pulmonary artery catheter waveform interpretation

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The pre-change trace (mean ~4 mmHg, small a/v waves) is the RA/CVP waveform, and the new pulsatile 28/3 mmHg trace with a steep upstroke and near-zero diastolic is the classic right ventricular waveform — the keyed answer (RV) is the single best answer. Distractors are genuinely inferior: PA would show the same systolic but an elevated diastolic (~8-12) with a dicrotic notch and narrower pulse pressure (the correct discriminator, accurately stated); wedge is a damped low a/v trace; RA and SVC are low venous waveforms. The detail that the balloon is inflated before the RV trace appears is consistent with correct flow-directed PAC technique (balloon inflated in the RA, then floated through the RV into the PA), so it introduces no error. The explanation's waveform sequence, the RV-ectopy clinical pearl, and the diastolic step-up teaching point are all accurate and current. REFERENCE: The reference_url (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482170/) resolves to StatPearls 'Pulmonary Artery Catheterization,' an authoritative, on-topic source that explicitly describes each right-heart segment having a distinctive waveform pattern used to localize the catheter tip — a direct match for the question's content.

stem: A 58-year-old woman with severe idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (baseline mean PA pressure 55 mmHg, on chronic inhaled treprostinil) is under general anesthesia for laparoscopic cholecystectomy. After CO2 insufflation she becomes acutely hypotensive (BP 70/45 mmHg), with rising central venous pressure, a falling end-tidal CO2, and SpO2 dropping to 86%. TEE shows a severely dilated, hypokinetic right ventricle with leftward bowing of the interventricular septum and a small, underfilled left ventricle. Which intervention is most appropriate to manage this pulmonary hypertensive crisis with acute right ventricular failure?

choices.text: Administer a 1-liter rapid crystalloid bolus to raise preload and 'fill' the failing right ventricle
choices.rationale: Incorrect — aggressive volume loading further distends the already overloaded RV, worsens septal bowing and LV underfilling, and typically worsens the hemodynamic collapse.

choices.text: Start a phenylephrine infusion as the sole agent because pure alpha-agonism is ideal in this setting
choices.rationale: Incorrect — while a vasopressor to restore coronary perfusion is reasonable, phenylephrine can reflexively raise pulmonary vascular resistance and provides no inotropic support for the failing RV, so it is not the best single answer.

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia with a propofol bolus and increase the volatile agent to blunt sympathetic tone
choices.rationale: Incorrect — a propofol bolus and deeper volatile anesthesia cause systemic vasodilation and myocardial depression that will deepen the hypotension and RV failure.

choices.text: Initiate inhaled nitric oxide (or inhaled prostacyclin), optimize oxygenation and ventilation to avoid hypoxia/hypercarbia/acidosis, and support systemic pressure and RV contractility with norepinephrine plus an inodilator
choices.rationale: Correct — selectively lowering pulmonary vascular resistance with an inhaled pulmonary vasodilator while correcting hypoxemia/acidosis and supporting coronary perfusion and inotropy directly reverses the pathophysiology of RV failure.

choices.text: Give a beta-blocker (esmolol) to slow the heart rate and improve right ventricular diastolic filling
choices.rationale: Incorrect — negative inotropy and chronotropy from a beta-blocker are dangerous in acute RV failure, where cardiac output is rate- and contractility-dependent.

explanation: In a pulmonary hypertensive crisis the right ventricle fails because afterload (pulmonary vascular resistance) acutely exceeds its capacity, producing RV dilation, septal shift that impairs LV filling, and systemic hypotension. Management targets the physiology directly: reduce PVR with a selective inhaled pulmonary vasodilator (inhaled nitric oxide or prostacyclin) and by aggressively correcting the reversible PVR-raising insults of hypoxemia, hypercarbia, and acidosis. Simultaneously, restore systemic (and thus right coronary) perfusion pressure and augment RV contractility — norepinephrine plus an inodilator such as milrinone or dobutamine is a common combination. Indiscriminate fluid loading and any negative inotrope worsen the failing RV.
subtopic: Pulmonary hypertension and acute right ventricular failure — intraoperative management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook pulmonary hypertensive crisis with acute RV failure precipitated by CO2 insufflation (hypercarbia/acidosis + raised intra-abdominal pressure raise PVR). Hemodynamics (hypotension, rising CVP, falling EtCO2 from reduced pulmonary blood flow/cardiac output, desaturation) and TEE findings (dilated hypokinetic RV, leftward septal bowing, underfilled LV) are classic. Keyed answer D is the single best answer: selective inhaled pulmonary vasodilator (iNO/inhaled prostacyclin) to lower PVR + correction of hypoxia/hypercarbia/acidosis + norepinephrine for coronary perfusion + inodilator (milrinone/dobutamine) for RV contractility is the guideline-concordant bundle and is the only option that improves the physiology. All distractors are genuinely inferior: A (1L bolus) overdistends the failing RV and worsens septal bowing; B (phenylephrine alone) gives no inotropy and can raise PVR; C (deepen anesthesia) causes vasodilation/myocardial depression; E (esmolol) imposes dangerous negative inotropy/chronotropy in rate- and contractility-dependent RV failure. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482463/ resolves to the correct StatPearls 'Pulmonary Hypertension' article (title matches reference_title). It is genuinely on-topic for PH/RV failure, not fabricated/404/mismatched. It is a general PH overview rather than a focused intraoperative-crisis source, but that is an acceptable, legitimate citation. Both clinical and reference are sound -> keep.

stem: A 24-year-old man with a known but previously asymptomatic accessory pathway (Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern on a prior ECG) presents for emergency appendectomy. During induction he develops an irregularly irregular wide-complex tachycardia at a ventricular rate of 220-260/min with beat-to-beat variation in QRS morphology. His blood pressure is 96/58 mmHg and he remains conscious. The rhythm is recognized as pre-excited atrial fibrillation conducting antegrade down the accessory pathway. Which agent is the most appropriate pharmacologic treatment?

choices.text: Intravenous adenosine
choices.rationale: Incorrect — by transiently blocking the AV node, adenosine can accelerate conduction down the accessory pathway and precipitate ventricular fibrillation in pre-excited atrial fibrillation.

choices.text: Intravenous diltiazem
choices.rationale: Incorrect — calcium channel blockers slow AV-nodal conduction, favoring rapid accessory-pathway conduction, and are contraindicated in pre-excited atrial fibrillation.

choices.text: Intravenous procainamide
choices.rationale: Correct — procainamide slows conduction and prolongs the refractory period of the accessory pathway, controlling the ventricular rate without the danger of AV-nodal blockade, and is the recommended agent for stable pre-excited atrial fibrillation.

choices.text: Intravenous metoprolol
choices.rationale: Incorrect — beta-blockers block the AV node and shift conduction toward the accessory pathway, risking acceleration to ventricular fibrillation.

choices.text: Intravenous digoxin
choices.rationale: Incorrect — digoxin slows AV-nodal conduction and may shorten accessory-pathway refractoriness, both of which can dangerously increase the ventricular response.

explanation: Pre-excited atrial fibrillation in WPW is a potentially lethal rhythm because the fibrillatory impulses bypass the rate-limiting AV node and conduct rapidly down the accessory pathway, risking degeneration into ventricular fibrillation. The classic teaching is to AVOID all AV-nodal blocking drugs — adenosine, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and digoxin — because blocking the node funnels more conduction down the accessory pathway and can accelerate the ventricular rate.
